name: Semaglutide
summary: A long-acting GLP-1 receptor agonist FDA-approved for type 2 diabetes and chronic weight management, producing roughly 15 percent mean body-weight reduction.
mechanism: Mimics native GLP-1, binding GLP-1 receptors to stimulate glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon, slow gastric emptying, and reduce appetite via hypothalamic pathways. A DPP-4-resistant substitution plus a fatty-acid chain that binds albumin extends the half-life to about 7 days, allowing once-weekly dosing. Receptors in multiple organs explain its broad metabolic, cardiovascular, and liver effects.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once weekly, titrated 0.25 mg to 2.4 mg over about 16 weeks (0.25 to 0.5 to 1.0 to 1.7 to 2.4 mg); 0.5 to 1 mg weekly typical for diabetes and cardiovascular indications. Oral form runs 3 mg to 25 mg daily.
cycle: Ongoing continuous therapy as prescribed; steady state reached after about 4 to 5 weeks of weekly dosing, with no off-period.
sideEffects: Mainly gastrointestinal (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation), worst during titration; gallbladder issues and rare pancreatitis. Carries a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors; contraindicated with personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2. Monitor HbA1c, weight, and watch for pancreatitis signs.
comparisons: Within GLP-1 and metabolic agents, semaglutide is the most established with the broadest approval history and safety data, but produces less weight loss than dual agonist tirzepatide or triple agonist retatrutide. Adding amylin analog cagrilintide (CagriSema) pushes results above semaglutide alone.

name: Tirzepatide
summary: A first-in-class dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist FDA-approved for type 2 diabetes and chronic weight management, with up to roughly 22 percent body-weight reduction in trials.
mechanism: Activates both GIP and GLP-1 receptors, producing glucose-dependent insulin stimulation, glucagon suppression, delayed gastric emptying, and central satiety signaling. The added GIP component enhances insulin sensitivity and metabolic effects beyond GLP-1 alone. A fatty-acid moiety gives an approximately 5-day half-life supporting once-weekly dosing.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once weekly, titrated in 2.5 mg steps every 4 weeks: 2.5 to 5 to 7.5 to 10 to 12.5 to 15 mg. 2.5 mg is a tolerability dose; 5, 10, and 15 mg are the approved maintenance doses.
cycle: Ongoing therapy as prescribed; stay 4 weeks at each titration step (longer if GI effects flare) and continue at the chosen maintenance dose with no off-period.
sideEffects: Gastrointestinal effects (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation), gallbladder problems, rare pancreatitis, and small heart-rate increases. Boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors; avoid with personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2. Monitor HbA1c, weight, and lipase if symptomatic.
comparisons: Tirzepatide outperforms the GLP-1-only semaglutide on weight loss and HbA1c, sitting between it and the still-investigational triple agonist retatrutide. Versus dual GLP-1/glucagon agents like survodutide and mazdutide, tirzepatide has the largest approved evidence base.

name: Retatrutide
summary: An investigational once-weekly triple GLP-1/GIP/glucagon receptor agonist (LY3437943) that produced the highest weight loss recorded for an obesity drug, about 24 percent at 48 weeks in Phase 2.
mechanism: Simultaneously activates GLP-1 receptors for appetite suppression, GIP receptors for insulin sensitivity, and glucagon receptors for increased energy expenditure and hepatic fat oxidation. The added glucagon agonism drives thermogenesis and dramatic liver-fat reduction beyond dual agonists. Half-life is roughly 6 days, enabling weekly dosing.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once weekly, climbing every 4 weeks. Phase 3 TRIUMPH schedule: 1 to 2 to 4 to 6 to 9 to 12 mg (4, 9, and 12 mg are target maintenance doses); Phase 2 NEJM schedule: 1 to 2 to 4 to 8 to 12 mg with no 6 mg step. Maximum studied dose 12 mg weekly.
cycle: Trial protocols run 48 weeks (Phase 2) to 68 to 72 weeks (Phase 3), 4 weeks per dose step, extending a step by 2 to 4 weeks if GI symptoms persist; intended as continuous therapy, not cycled.
sideEffects: Triple agonism means pronounced GI effects (nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea each up to about 25 percent at 12 mg), heart-rate increases especially in the first 24 weeks, and a dysesthesia safety signal. Investigational and not FDA-approved as of 2026; counterfeits circulate. Avoid with medullary thyroid carcinoma history or MEN2; watch for pancreatitis and excessive weight loss.
comparisons: Retatrutide currently leads its category on weight loss, exceeding tirzepatide and semaglutide, at the cost of heavier GI burden and unproven long-term safety. It is one mechanism beyond tirzepatide's dual agonism and is frequently discussed alongside dual GLP-1/glucagon agents survodutide and mazdutide.

name: Cagrilintide
summary: A long-acting lipidated amylin analog (AM833) and dual amylin/calcitonin receptor agonist studied for weight management, most notably in the CagriSema combination with semaglutide.
mechanism: Activates amylin and calcitonin receptors to enhance satiety, slow gastric emptying, and improve insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism. Acting on a pathway distinct from GLP-1 makes it complementary to incretin drugs. A lipid side chain gives an approximately 7 to 8 day half-life for once-weekly dosing; it requires acidic pH (about 3.5 to 4.5) on reconstitution to prevent fibril formation.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once weekly. Monotherapy titrates from about 0.6 mg up to a studied maximum of 4.5 mg weekly; in CagriSema it titrates with semaglutide 0.25 to 0.5 to 1.0 to 1.7 to 2.4 mg of each over 16 weeks, then 2.4 mg/2.4 mg maintenance.
cycle: Continuous long-term weekly therapy; titration steps last about 4 weeks each, held longer if GI effects are not tolerated, with no off-period.
sideEffects: Dose-dependent gastrointestinal effects (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea), worst early; anti-cagrilintide antibodies develop in roughly 46 to 73 percent but do not appear to affect efficacy. Investigational and not yet FDA-approved as standalone; not for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Combining with retatrutide compounds GI risk; monitor for pancreatitis and injection-site reactions.
comparisons: Cagrilintide is the leading amylin analog and uniquely non-incretin in this category, modest on its own but additive when stacked: CagriSema beats semaglutide alone and rivals tirzepatide. Unlike pramlintide it is long-acting and weekly.

name: Mazdutide
summary: An investigational dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist (an oxyntomodulin analog) studied for obesity and type 2 diabetes, with up to roughly 20 percent body-weight loss in Phase 3.
mechanism: Dual agonist: GLP-1 activation stimulates insulin, suppresses glucagon, and slows gastric emptying, while glucagon receptor activation increases energy expenditure and thermogenesis, with GLP-1 counteracting glucagon's glucose-raising effect. Fatty-acid conjugation extends the half-life to about 6 to 8 days for once-weekly dosing.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once weekly, with titration toward target doses: e.g. 1.5 to 3 mg (3 mg target), up to 3 to 6 to 9 mg (9 mg target); 3 to 4.5 mg weekly typical for mild-to-moderate type 2 diabetes.
cycle: Continuous; trials run about 48 to 60-plus weeks for optimal results, titrating up over the first weeks then maintaining, with no off-period.
sideEffects: Gastrointestinal effects (nausea, diarrhea, vomiting), increased heart rate (about 5 to 17 bpm), and possible mood changes. Investigational; avoid with medullary thyroid carcinoma history or MEN2, and in pregnancy or breastfeeding. Watch for pancreatitis and gallbladder symptoms; monitor glucose and HbA1c.
comparisons: Mazdutide is a GLP-1/glucagon dual agonist like survodutide; reported trial data suggest superiority over semaglutide on weight and glycemic control, but it has far less approved evidence than tirzepatide and lacks the glucagon-driven thermogenic edge that retatrutide adds GIP on top of.

name: Survodutide
summary: An investigational dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist (BI 456906) studied for obesity, MASH, and type 2 diabetes, with about 15 percent mean weight loss at 46 weeks.
mechanism: Balanced dual agonism (EC50 roughly 0.52 nM at the glucagon receptor and 0.33 nM at GLP-1): GLP-1 reduces appetite and slows gastric emptying while glucagon receptor activation increases energy expenditure and hepatic fat oxidation, driving strong liver-fat clearance. Fatty-acid acylation supports once-weekly dosing.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once weekly. Conservative start 0.6 mg titrated over about 24 weeks at 4-week intervals; standard obesity protocol 3.6 to 6.0 mg weekly; MASH 2.4 to 4.8 mg; type 2 diabetes 0.3 to 2.7 mg weekly.
cycle: Continuous weekly therapy with a long graded titration (around 24 weeks) then maintenance; no off-period.
sideEffects: High rates of gastrointestinal effects (nausea about 40 to 66 percent, diarrhea 25 to 49 percent, vomiting 15 to 41 percent), small heart-rate increase, and possible tachycardia. Investigational; not for pregnancy or breastfeeding, use contraception during treatment. Watch for pancreatitis and gallbladder symptoms; monitor glucose, especially with insulin or sulfonylureas.
comparisons: Survodutide is a GLP-1/glucagon dual agonist like mazdutide, with notable MASH efficacy; reported data put it ahead of semaglutide on weight in some comparisons but behind tirzepatide's approved track record, and it carries a heavier nausea burden than most GLP-1-only agents.

name: Liraglutide
summary: A once-daily GLP-1 receptor agonist FDA-approved for type 2 diabetes (Victoza) and chronic weight management (Saxenda), the first-generation predecessor to semaglutide.
mechanism: Binds GLP-1 receptors to increase glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon, slow gastric emptying, and reduce appetite via central pathways. A fatty-acid linker provides albumin binding and an approximately 13-hour half-life, requiring daily rather than weekly injection.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once daily. Weight management (Saxenda): titrate 0.6 mg weekly up to 3.0 mg daily. Diabetes (Victoza): 0.6 mg, then 1.2 mg, up to 1.8 mg daily.
cycle: Ongoing continuous daily therapy as prescribed; no off-period.
sideEffects: Gastrointestinal effects (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea), gallbladder disease, rare pancreatitis, and increased heart rate. Boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors; avoid with medullary thyroid carcinoma history or MEN2. Monitor HbA1c and weight; watch for pancreatitis.
comparisons: Liraglutide produces less weight loss (around 5 to 8 percent) than weekly semaglutide and far less than tirzepatide or retatrutide, and requires daily dosing, so it is now largely superseded within the GLP-1 and metabolic category despite its long safety record.

name: Cagri+Sema Blend
summary: A fixed combination of the amylin analog cagrilintide and the GLP-1 agonist semaglutide (CagriSema), studied as a once-weekly injection for obesity and type 2 diabetes.
mechanism: Pairs two complementary satiety pathways: semaglutide drives GLP-1 receptor agonism (insulin secretion, glucagon suppression, slowed gastric emptying, central appetite reduction) while cagrilintide adds amylin/calcitonin receptor agonism (enhanced satiety, slowed gastric emptying, improved insulin sensitivity). The additive effect yields greater weight loss than either component alone; both are long-acting and lipidated for weekly dosing.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once weekly. Co-titrated over 16 weeks at 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 1.7, then 2.4 mg of each compound, with a 2.4 mg cagrilintide / 2.4 mg semaglutide maintenance dose from week 17. Hold a step longer if GI effects are poorly tolerated.
cycle: Continuous long-term weekly therapy; 4 weeks per titration step then ongoing maintenance, no off-period.
sideEffects: Dominated by gastrointestinal effects (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation), which can exceed semaglutide alone given the dual mechanism; gallbladder issues, rare pancreatitis, anti-cagrilintide antibodies. Investigational combination filed with the FDA; avoid with medullary thyroid carcinoma history or MEN2 and in pregnancy. Monitor HbA1c, weight, hydration, and pancreatitis signs.
comparisons: CagriSema beats semaglutide monotherapy on weight loss (about 20 percent versus 15 percent in REDEFINE 1) and competes with tirzepatide, while adding amylin-specific GI tolerability considerations not seen with single-agent GLP-1 therapy.

name: Reta+Cagri Blend
summary: An off-label research stack pairing the triple agonist retatrutide with the amylin analog cagrilintide, aiming to combine maximal incretin/glucagon effects with amylin-driven satiety.
mechanism: Retatrutide contributes GLP-1 (appetite suppression), GIP (insulin sensitivity), and glucagon (energy expenditure, hepatic fat oxidation) agonism; cagrilintide adds a separate amylin/calcitonin satiety pathway. The combination layers two non-overlapping appetite mechanisms on top of glucagon-mediated thermogenesis, in theory pushing weight loss beyond either alone. Both are weekly, lipidated peptides.
dosing: No established protocol exists; this combination is not in formal trials. In practice researchers titrate each component slowly and independently per its own schedule (retatrutide from 1 mg toward up to 12 mg weekly; cagrilintide from low doses toward 2.4 to 4.5 mg weekly), subcutaneous, once weekly. Sources do not report validated combined doses.
cycle: No standardized cycle; would follow each component's continuous weekly titration-then-maintenance pattern, with extended steps if GI symptoms appear.
sideEffects: Compounded gastrointestinal toxicity is the main concern, since retatrutide already causes nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea up to about 25 percent at top dose and cagrilintide adds its own GI burden; also heart-rate increases, retatrutide's dysesthesia signal, and risk of excessive weight loss. Both components are investigational and unapproved; combining them is experimental and not recommended without specialist supervision. Avoid with medullary thyroid carcinoma history or MEN2 and in pregnancy; monitor weight, HbA1c, hydration, and pancreatitis signs.
comparisons: Conceptually the most aggressive weight-loss stack in the category, going beyond CagriSema by swapping semaglutide for the triple agonist retatrutide; potency comes at the cost of stacked GI side effects and a complete absence of combination safety data versus single-agent or approved options like tirzepatide.

name: Reta+Tirz Blend
summary: An off-label research stack combining the triple agonist retatrutide with the dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist tirzepatide; mechanistically redundant and primarily seen in informal use rather than clinical practice.
mechanism: Both peptides activate GLP-1 and GIP receptors, so the combination largely overlaps; retatrutide adds glucagon receptor agonism for energy expenditure and hepatic fat oxidation, which tirzepatide lacks. Because the receptor targets so heavily coincide, the practical effect is mostly higher combined incretin tone rather than a true additive new pathway. Both are weekly, fatty-acid-modified peptides.
dosing: No validated protocol; not studied in trials. If used, each is titrated independently and conservatively per its own schedule (tirzepatide 2.5 mg upward to 15 mg weekly; retatrutide 1 mg upward to 12 mg weekly), subcutaneous, once weekly. Sources report no combined dose figures and the overlap argues against full doses of both.
cycle: No standardized cycle; would follow continuous weekly titration-then-maintenance for each, with longer steps if GI symptoms emerge.
sideEffects: Markedly amplified gastrointestinal effects (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation) from doubling up incretin agonism, plus heart-rate increases, retatrutide's dysesthesia signal, and risk of excessive weight loss. Retatrutide is investigational and unapproved; stacking it on tirzepatide is experimental, mechanistically redundant, and not recommended. Avoid with medullary thyroid carcinoma history or MEN2 and in pregnancy; monitor HbA1c, weight, hydration, and pancreatitis signs.
comparisons: Offers little over retatrutide alone (which already covers GLP-1, GIP, and glucagon) while adding the side-effect load of two agents; a complementary stack like retatrutide plus the amylin analog cagrilintide or simply CagriSema is the more rational combination within this category.

name: Tesamorelin
summary: An FDA-approved synthetic GHRH analog (Egrifta) used to reduce excess visceral fat in HIV-associated lipodystrophy, with selective visceral fat targeting and liver-fat reduction.
mechanism: Binds pituitary GHRH receptors to stimulate pulsatile endogenous growth hormone release, which in turn raises IGF-1 and drives lipolysis preferentially in visceral adipose tissue while sparing subcutaneous fat. Because it works through the body's own GH pulse rather than supplying exogenous GH, the physiologic feedback loop is largely preserved.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once daily, typically in the evening. HIV lipodystrophy (approved): 1.4 mg daily. Reported research uses: about 2 mg daily for visceral fat or NAFLD, and 1 to 2 mg roughly 5 to 7 times weekly for body-composition or anti-aging goals.
cycle: Used continuously; benefits accrue over months and reverse on discontinuation. NAFLD protocols reported around 12 months; cognitive-study use about 20 weeks. No fixed on/off pattern in the sources.
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions (about 17 percent), arthralgia (about 13 percent), fluid retention, and glucose intolerance. Contraindicated with active malignancy, pituitary disorders, and pregnancy. Monitor IGF-1 (discontinue if more than 2 SD above normal with acromegaly symptoms), HbA1c/fasting glucose, and watch for new masses.
comparisons: Among GH secretagogues, tesamorelin is the only FDA-approved one and has the strongest visceral-fat and liver-fat evidence, but it requires daily injection unlike weekly stacks; it is often paired with a ghrelin-mimetic such as ipamorelin to amplify the GH pulse, whereas plain GHRH fragments like sermorelin are shorter-acting and less potent.

name: HGH 191AA
summary: Recombinant human growth hormone (somatropin), a 191-amino-acid polypeptide FDA-approved for pediatric and adult growth hormone deficiency and HIV-associated wasting; widely used off-label for body composition and anti-aging.
mechanism: Binds GH receptors on target tissues, triggering JAK2-STAT5 signaling; direct effects include lipolysis, protein synthesis, and metabolic regulation, while indirect effects via hepatic IGF-1 production promote tissue growth and anabolism. Subcutaneous half-life is roughly 3 hours, but biological effects persist far longer through IGF-1.
dosing: Subcutaneous, once daily (sometimes split twice daily). Medical GHD: 0.15 to 0.3 mg/day starting, 0.4 to 0.8 mg/day maintenance. Reported off-label use: 1 to 2 IU/day (about 0.33 to 0.67 mg) for wellness, 2 to 4 IU/day for recomposition, and 4 to 8 IU/day for performance (flagged higher-risk). Morning fasted dosing emphasizes fat loss; evening mimics the natural nocturnal pulse.
cycle: Medical use is continuous. Off-label users commonly run multi-month blocks (often 3 to 6 months or longer); the sources do not prescribe a standard on/off pattern, and higher doses carry higher risk.
sideEffects: Fluid retention, joint pain and stiffness, carpal tunnel syndrome, headaches, and numbness or tingling; higher doses can cause insulin resistance, gynecomastia, and hypothyroid symptoms. Contraindicated in active cancer, acute critical illness, and pregnancy. Monitor IGF-1, fasting glucose/HbA1c, thyroid function, and watch for new masses or vision changes; supraphysiologic dosing for non-medical use is higher-risk.
comparisons: Direct recombinant HGH gives the most potent and predictable GH/IGF-1 elevation in this category, but it bypasses physiologic feedback and carries more side effects than secretagogues like tesamorelin, sermorelin, or ipamorelin, which raise GH only within the body's own pulsatile limits.

name: HGH Fragment 176-191
summary: A 16-amino-acid C-terminal fragment of human growth hormone corresponding to residues 176-191, used in research as a targeted fat-loss (lipolytic) peptide. AOD-9604 is a modified version with an added N-terminal tyrosine.
mechanism: Mimics the lipolytic region of human GH, stimulating fat breakdown and inhibiting lipogenesis through beta-3 adrenergic receptor pathways. It does not bind the growth hormone receptor, so it does not raise IGF-1 or disrupt glucose/insulin metabolism. The fragment selectively targets adipose tissue to promote fat oxidation.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: subcutaneous injection, 250mcg standard or up to 500mcg for enhanced fat loss, dosed twice daily on an empty stomach (morning fasted and pre-bed); a conservative protocol uses 250mcg once daily fasted. Avoid food for at least 30 minutes after injection.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks continuous, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; injection-site irritation and mild headache (especially early) are most common. Not FDA approved (research chemical only) and WADA prohibited. Contraindicated in pregnancy/breastfeeding and active or prior malignancy; monitor blood glucose if combined with insulin.
comparisons: Within GH-related fat-loss compounds, HGH Fragment 176-191 is the parent sequence of AOD-9604 and shares its mechanism, so combining the two is redundant. Unlike full HGH or GH secretagogues (sermorelin, ipamorelin, CJC-1295), it does not raise IGF-1 or build muscle, acting purely on lipolysis.

name: GHRP-2
summary: GHRP-2 (pralmorelin) is a potent synthetic growth hormone-releasing hexapeptide that stimulates the pituitary to release endogenous GH; it was originally developed as a diagnostic agent for GH deficiency.
mechanism: Acts as a synthetic ghrelin mimetic, binding the growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHS-R1a) in the hypothalamus and pituitary, raising cAMP and driving pulsatile GH release without suppressing natural feedback. It works synergistically with GHRH analogs to amplify GH secretion beyond either alone, with mild orexigenic (appetite) effects and a smaller cortisol/prolactin response than GHRP-6.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: subcutaneous injection on an empty stomach, 100-150mcg for GH optimization or 200-300mcg for enhanced results, 2-3x daily; with a GHRH peptide, 100mcg each; a pre-sleep protocol uses 100-200mcg before bed.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Increased appetite (less than GHRP-6), mild water retention, tingling/numbness in extremities, post-injection tiredness, mild headache; cortisol elevation mainly at doses above ~300mcg. Not FDA approved and WADA prohibited; contraindicated in active/prior cancer, pregnancy, pituitary disorders, diabetic retinopathy; monitor blood glucose in diabetics.
comparisons: Among GHRPs, GHRP-2 is 2-3x more potent than GHRP-6 at releasing GH but causes far less hunger; it is stronger than ipamorelin but less selective (more prolactin/cortisol). It is typically used alone or stacked with a GHRH analog rather than with other GHRPs.

name: GHRP-6
summary: GHRP-6 is one of the first synthetic growth hormone-releasing hexapeptides, stimulating endogenous GH release via the ghrelin receptor; it is best known for its strong appetite-stimulating effect.
mechanism: Binds the growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHS-R1a), the same receptor as ghrelin, triggering a rapid pulse of pituitary GH which then drives hepatic IGF-1 secretion. It also potently stimulates ghrelin release, producing marked hunger, and shows cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory effects in research models.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: subcutaneous injection on an empty stomach, 100mcg for GH optimization or 200-300mcg for enhanced results, 2-3x daily; with a GHRH peptide, 100mcg each; for appetite/bulking, 100-200mcg before meals.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Intense hunger (its hallmark effect), water retention, tingling/numbness, post-injection lethargy, mild headache; carbohydrate intake blunts GH release. Not FDA approved and WADA prohibited; contraindicated in active/prior cancer, pregnancy, pituitary disorders; use with caution in diabetes and monitor blood glucose.
comparisons: GHRP-6 is less potent than GHRP-2 or hexarelin for GH release and causes much more appetite stimulation, which makes it favored for bulking but a poor choice for cutting. Compared with ipamorelin it is cruder (more hunger and side effects); like other GHRPs it is best stacked with a GHRH analog rather than another GHRP.

name: Sermorelin
summary: Sermorelin is a synthetic 29-amino-acid analog of growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH 1-29) that stimulates the body's own pulsatile GH production; it was FDA-approved (Geref) for pediatric GH deficiency until the manufacturer discontinued it in 2008 for manufacturing reasons.
mechanism: Binds GHRH receptors on pituitary somatotrophs to trigger physiological, pulsatile GH release while preserving the hypothalamic-pituitary axis and somatostatin negative feedback, which gives a natural ceiling on GH levels. Short half-life (~11-12 minutes) means the GH pulse mirrors the body's natural nocturnal surge, so it is dosed nightly.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com and peptidedosingprotocols.com: subcutaneous injection, 100-500mcg per dose (commonly 200-300mcg, titrated 100 to 200 to 300 to 400-500mcg), once nightly at bedtime on an empty stomach, 5-7 nights/week; some protocols co-inject 200mcg with a GHRP.
cycle: Typically 3-6 months continuous with lab monitoring at baseline, 6 weeks and 12 weeks; some use 5-nights-on/2-off pulse protocols, per the sources.
sideEffects: Mild injection-site reactions in roughly 1 in 6 users; less common headache, flushing, dizziness, dysphagia, drowsiness, hives; anti-GRF antibody formation is frequent but usually not clinically limiting, and about 6.5% developed hypothyroidism in trials so thyroid labs (plus IGF-1) are monitored. Not currently FDA-approved; contraindicated in active malignancy, pituitary tumors and pregnancy.
comparisons: Among GHRH analogs, sermorelin is the shortest-acting (nightly dosing) with the longest historical clinical track record but limited modern adult RCT data; tesamorelin has stronger modern FDA-backed evidence for visceral fat reduction and CJC-1295 DAC offers far longer dosing intervals. It is commonly stacked with a GHRP (e.g., ipamorelin or GHRP-2) for synergistic GH release.

name: Ipamorelin
summary: Ipamorelin is a selective pentapeptide growth hormone secretagogue (ghrelin mimetic) that triggers clean, pulsatile GH release; it is prized for raising GH without the cortisol, prolactin or strong appetite effects of other GHRPs.
mechanism: Binds the GHS-R1a (ghrelin) receptor on the pituitary, producing a short natural GH pulse (peaking ~30-40 minutes post-injection) that drives downstream IGF-1, while leaving cortisol, ACTH and prolactin essentially unchanged even at high doses. Its ~2-hour half-life means dosing is built around clean individual pulses, and bedtime dosing aligns with a natural dip in somatostatin.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com and peptidedosingprotocols.com: subcutaneous injection on an empty stomach, 100-300mcg per shot (community protocols titrate 100 to 200 to 200-300mcg), 1-3x daily; 1x at bedtime is the common base protocol, 2-3x daily for performance; GH release plateaus above ~300mcg per shot.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks on then 4 weeks off (some clinician protocols extend to week 16) to keep the GH receptor responsive, per the sources.
sideEffects: Generally mild and short-lived: mild headache, light water retention/bloating, mild appetite increase (less than GHRP-6), tingling in hands or feet early in a cycle; higher doses or longer use can bring joint stiffness, fatigue or carpal-tunnel-type symptoms. Not FDA approved (Phase II for postoperative ileus failed) and WADA prohibited; avoid in active/recent cancer, uncontrolled diabetes, severe heart disease, pregnancy/breastfeeding and adolescents; some practitioners monitor fasting glucose, HbA1c and IGF-1.
comparisons: Ipamorelin is the most selective GHRP, with a cleaner hormonal profile and longer half-life (~2h) than GHRP-2 or GHRP-6 (~20-30 min) at similar doses, though slightly less raw GH-releasing potency than GHRP-2 or hexarelin. It is most often paired with a GHRH analog like CJC-1295 (no DAC) or sermorelin rather than stacked with another GHRP.

name: CJC-1295 DAC
summary: CJC-1295 with DAC is a long-acting GHRH analog engineered with a Drug Affinity Complex that binds serum albumin, extending its half-life to 6-8 days for sustained GH/IGF-1 elevation on a weekly dosing schedule.
mechanism: The DAC moiety covalently binds endogenous albumin, prolonging circulation from minutes to days and providing continuous GHRH receptor stimulation on pituitary somatotrophs. This sustains elevated GH and IGF-1 while studies show pulsatile GH secretion is largely preserved; mean GH rose ~46% and IGF-1 ~45% in clinical work, with trough GH increased about 7.5-fold.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: subcutaneous injection, 1mg once weekly (conservative anti-aging) up to 2mg once weekly (standard), or 1mg twice weekly (e.g. Mon/Thu) split dosing; a loading protocol uses 2mg the first week then 1mg weekly.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks; IGF-1 returns to baseline about 2-3 weeks after stopping, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Water retention, joint pain and carpal-tunnel symptoms are common from continuous GH elevation; watch for blood glucose dysregulation and signs of acromegaly (jaw/hand/feet growth) with high doses. Not FDA approved; contraindicated with diabetes history, cancer history and predisposition to sleep apnea. Do not combine with the non-DAC version, other GHRH analogs (tesamorelin), MK-677 or HGH.
comparisons: Versus CJC-1295 no-DAC, the DAC variant trades natural pulsatility for convenient weekly dosing but carries more side effects (sustained elevation stresses joints and connective tissue) and costs 2-3x more. Versus sermorelin or tesamorelin it is far longer-acting; it is best paired with a pulsatile GHRP rather than another GHRH analog.

name: CJC-1295 no-DAC
summary: CJC-1295 without DAC (Modified GRF 1-29) is a short-acting GHRH analog with about 4x the receptor affinity of native GHRH, producing pulsatile GH release that resembles natural physiology; it requires multiple daily injections.
mechanism: Binds GHRH receptors on pituitary somatotrophs, raising cAMP and triggering physiological GH pulses. The brief 30-minute to ~2-hour half-life (versus 6-8 days for the DAC version) preserves natural pulsatility and avoids receptor desensitization, and it synergizes strongly with GHRP peptides through a complementary receptor pathway.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com and peptidedosingprotocols.com: subcutaneous injection on an empty stomach, 100-300mcg per injection (commonly ~100mcg), 2-3x daily (morning, optional post-workout, bedtime); ~100-200mcg once at bedtime for sleep; often co-dosed with a GHRP.
cycle: Typically 12-16 weeks, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses; temporary facial flushing/warmth for ~5-10 minutes after injection is common, with persistent joint pain, carpal-tunnel symptoms, water retention/edema, headaches or vision changes being stop signs. Not FDA approved; contraindicated in active cancer, diabetic retinopathy, severe kidney disease and pregnancy/breastfeeding.
comparisons: Versus CJC-1295 with DAC, the no-DAC version preserves natural pulsatile GH and has fewer side effects but demands 2-3 injections per day instead of one weekly shot. It is the classic GHRH partner for a GHRP stack (e.g. with ipamorelin), and is comparable in role to sermorelin but with higher receptor affinity.

name: CJC-1295 + Ipamorelin Blend
summary: A popular dual-pathway blend combining CJC-1295 (a GHRH analog) with ipamorelin (a selective GHRP), used in research for GH optimization, recovery, body composition and sleep.
mechanism: CJC-1295 activates GHRH receptors on pituitary somatotrophs while ipamorelin activates the GHS-R1a (ghrelin) receptor; the two complementary pathways potentiate each other's cAMP signaling, producing a larger GH pulse than either peptide alone. Ipamorelin uniquely avoids cortisol and prolactin elevation, so the combination preserves a natural pulsatile rhythm with a clean side-effect profile.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: subcutaneous injection, 200mcg of each peptide for general health, 250mcg each for performance, 300mcg each for recovery, once daily (evening preferred, ~30-60 min before bed, fasted); a conservative approach uses 150mcg each 5 days/week. Available as pre-blended vials (commonly a 1:1 ratio) or separate vials.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks on, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Water retention and joint swelling, carpal-tunnel symptoms (numbness/tingling), mild blood glucose elevation and injection-site irritation; stop for persistent joint pain, glucose dysregulation or unusual fatigue/mood changes. Components are not FDA approved as a blend (tesamorelin and ipamorelin individually are investigational/limited approval) and the combination lacks peer-reviewed clinical validation; contraindicated in cancer history, severe diabetes and nerve-compression disorders.
comparisons: Compared with running CJC-1295 or ipamorelin alone, the blend gives synergistic GH release through two receptors at once; it is generally cleaner than a CJC-1295 + GHRP-6 or + GHRP-2 stack because ipamorelin does not raise cortisol, prolactin or hunger. Beginners often prefer separate vials over the pre-blend for dose flexibility.

name: Hexarelin
summary: Hexarelin (examorelin) is one of the most potent synthetic growth hormone-releasing hexapeptides, stimulating GH via the ghrelin receptor and also showing distinct cardioprotective effects.
mechanism: Binds the growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHS-R1a) in the pituitary and hypothalamus, mimicking ghrelin to drive somatotrophs to release GH, with strong synergy when co-administered with GHRH (GH response greater than the sum of either alone). It has slight dose-dependent effects on prolactin, ACTH and cortisol, and cardioprotective actions mediated through the CD36 receptor and calcium-channel modulation in cardiomyocytes; it is also resistant to proteolytic enzymes.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: subcutaneous or intramuscular injection on an empty stomach, 100mcg for GH optimization or 200mcg for enhanced results, 2-3x daily; with a GHRH peptide, 100mcg each; for cardioprotection, 100-200mcg 1-2x daily.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks, with cycling advised to prevent desensitization, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Increased appetite (less than GHRP-6), water retention, tingling/numbness, post-injection tiredness, mild headache; cortisol/prolactin can rise mildly and dose-dependently. Not FDA approved and WADA prohibited; contraindicated in active/prior cancer, pregnancy and pituitary disorders. Hexarelin is also more prone than other GHRPs to receptor desensitization with continuous use.
comparisons: Hexarelin is the strongest GHRP for raw GH release and shows uniquely strong GHRH synergy, but it desensitizes the GHS receptor faster and has slightly more cortisol/prolactin/appetite effects than ipamorelin. It causes less hunger than GHRP-6; like other GHRPs it is best paired with a GHRH analog rather than another GHRP.

name: MK-677
summary: MK-677 (ibutamoren) is an orally active, non-peptide ghrelin receptor agonist that raises GH and IGF-1; its >60% oral bioavailability and ~24-hour half-life make it the only convenient once-daily oral GH secretagogue.
mechanism: Selectively binds GHS-R1a receptors in the hypothalamus and pituitary, triggering pulsatile GH release while maintaining natural circadian patterns; in trials this produced roughly a 97% increase in 24-hour GH secretion and 40-72% higher IGF-1. Downstream effects include improved REM sleep, increased fat-free mass and nitrogen retention, and increased bone-formation markers.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: oral, start at 12.5mg daily to assess tolerance and increase to 25mg daily, taken once daily at bedtime on an empty stomach.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks for body composition goals, per peptide-db.com (some longer-term studies ran 8-12 months).
sideEffects: Appetite stimulation (>50% of users), water retention (30-40%), lethargy (20-30%), fasting glucose elevation (~5-15 mg/dL) and decreased insulin sensitivity; significant testosterone suppression is unlikely on its own at up to 20mg/day but more likely above that. Not FDA approved and prohibited as a dietary supplement; contraindicated in heart disease/CHF, diabetes or pre-diabetes, active cancer and pregnancy/breastfeeding; monitor blood glucose and liver enzymes.
comparisons: MK-677 is unique among GH secretagogues for being oral and long-acting (24h), giving sustained baseline GH elevation rather than the discrete pulses of injectable GHRPs; that comes at the cost of more appetite, water retention, lethargy and glucose impact. It pairs well with a GHRH analog or GHRP for combined baseline-plus-pulse coverage but should not be stacked with CJC-1295 DAC or HGH.

name: Ipamorelin + Tesamorelin Blend
summary: A dual GH-secretagogue blend pairing tesamorelin (a GHRH analog FDA-approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy) with ipamorelin (a selective GHRP), aimed at synergistic GH release with emphasis on visceral fat loss and recovery.
mechanism: Tesamorelin directly stimulates GHRH receptors on pituitary somatotrophs to trigger pulsatile GH synthesis and secretion, while ipamorelin activates the GHS-R1a (ghrelin) receptor to amplify those GH pulses without significantly raising cortisol or prolactin. Engaging both the GHRH and GHRP pathways produces synergistic GH release greater than either component alone, while preserving natural feedback; tesamorelin specifically reduces visceral adipose tissue.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: subcutaneous injection on an empty stomach (evening/before bed preferred, avoid food 2-3h before and 30-60 min after), 200-400mcg total for the standard 5mg/5mg blend, 300-500mcg total for the 10mg/3mg (higher-tesamorelin) blend, once daily; advanced users 200-300mcg per dose twice daily. Common formulations are 5mg/5mg (1:1) or 10mg/3mg.
cycle: Typically 8-16 weeks continuous, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions, transient water retention, tingling/numbness in extremities, joint stiffness and increased hunger (the ipamorelin component); stop for severe injection-site reactions, significant edema, glucose dysregulation, severe joint pain or allergic reactions. Tesamorelin alone is FDA-approved (Egrifta) but research blends are not, and ipamorelin remains investigational; the specific combination has no published clinical trials. Contraindicated in active malignancy, diabetic retinopathy, pregnancy/breastfeeding and pituitary disorders.
comparisons: Versus a CJC-1295 + ipamorelin blend, this blend uses tesamorelin's stronger evidence base for visceral fat reduction; versus running tesamorelin alone it adds ipamorelin's clean pulsatile boost. It should not be combined with additional GHRH analogs (CJC-1295), other GHRPs or MK-677, as the dual-pathway design already covers both.

name: IGF-1 LR3
summary: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1) is a synthetic 83-amino-acid analog of insulin-like growth factor-1 with an N-terminal extension and R3 substitution, used in research for muscle hypertrophy; it has never been approved for human use.
mechanism: Acts as a full IGF-1 receptor agonist, activating the PI3K/Akt/mTOR and MAPK/ERK pathways that drive protein synthesis and satellite-cell-mediated muscle hypertrophy and hyperplasia. The N-terminal extension and arginine-3 substitution reduce binding to IGF binding proteins, keeping free circulating peptide elevated for an extended 20-30 hour half-life with roughly 3x the potency of native IGF-1.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com and peptidedosingprotocols.com: subcutaneous or intramuscular injection, 20-30mcg daily for beginners (post-workout), 40-60mcg intermediate, 80-100mcg advanced (single dose or split AM/PM), 10-20mcg women; consume 30-60g fast carbohydrate immediately after injection to prevent hypoglycemia and never inject before sleep.
cycle: Maximum 4-6 weeks with equal off-time, per peptide-db.com, to limit receptor desensitization and cumulative hypoglycemia risk.
sideEffects: Hypoglycemia is the critical risk and can last up to 20-30 hours given the long half-life; also water retention, joint stiffness, muscle soreness and increased workout pumps. Never approved for human use (research chemical only) and WADA prohibited; contraindicated with cancer history or undiagnosed growths (elevated IGF-1 is linked to several cancers) and may cause organ hypertrophy (heart, intestines). Do not combine with HGH or insulin.
comparisons: Among IGF and growth-factor compounds, IGF-1 LR3 is the longest-acting systemic IGF-1 analog, contrasting with IGF-1 DES and MGF/PEG-MGF which act more locally or briefly; its long half-life means more sustained anabolism but a much longer and more dangerous hypoglycemia window. Unlike GH secretagogues that raise IGF-1 indirectly via natural feedback, it floods the IGF-1 receptor directly with no built-in ceiling.

name: IGF-1 DES
summary: A truncated 67-amino-acid IGF-1 analog missing the first three N-terminal residues, used in research for localized muscle hypertrophy via site-specific post-workout injection.
mechanism: It activates the IGF-1 receptor and downstream PI3K/Akt/mTOR and MAPK/ERK signaling, driving protein synthesis, satellite cell proliferation and hyperplasia. Lacking the Gly-Pro-Glu tripeptide, it does not bind IGF binding proteins, so the full dose stays bioactive — roughly 10x the potency of native IGF-1. Its very short 20-30 minute half-life confines activity to the injection site.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com, 20-30 mcg (beginner) up to 50 mcg (advanced) injected intramuscularly directly into the trained muscle immediately post-workout; consume fast carbohydrates afterward to offset hypoglycemia.
cycle: 4-6 weeks maximum, then a break before any further use.
sideEffects: Hypoglycemia (must eat carbs after injection), localized swelling/soreness, lightheadedness; risk of disproportionate muscle growth with repeated site use and theoretical cancer-promotion concern. Not approved for human use — research chemical only, WADA-prohibited; contraindicated with cancer history, diabetes, or concurrent insulin.
comparisons: Versus IGF-1 LR3, DES is far shorter-acting and is chosen specifically for localized rather than systemic effects — the two are not stacked. It is more potent gram-for-gram than native IGF-1 or MGF, but its action is brief and tied to injection timing and site.

name: MGF
summary: Mechano growth factor is a 24-amino-acid C-terminal peptide from the IGF-1Ec splice variant, used in research to activate muscle satellite cells for localized repair after mechanical stress.
mechanism: MGF binds its receptor on dormant muscle satellite (stem) cells and stimulates MAPK/ERK signaling, promoting their proliferation and fusion to damaged fibers, plus enhanced protein synthesis. Its E-peptide domain acts distinctly from mature IGF-1. The ~5-7 minute half-life means effects are concentrated at the injection site.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com, 100-300 mcg daily, injected intramuscularly into the worked muscle immediately post-workout (some protocols titrate from 100 up to 300 mcg over weeks).
cycle: Typically run in blocks of several weeks alongside training cycles; no fixed standardized on/off pattern reported, with breaks between cycles.
sideEffects: Injection site soreness and mild fatigue; discontinue for any lumps, unusual tissue changes, persistent headaches, vision changes, or hypoglycemia signs. Contraindicated with any cancer history, uncontrolled diabetes, and pregnancy; not an approved human drug.
comparisons: MGF is essentially the same active peptide as PEG-MGF but unpegylated, so it acts only locally and briefly — combining the two is redundant. Unlike IGF-1 LR3, it targets satellite-cell activation rather than sustained systemic IGF signaling.

name: PEG-MGF
summary: A PEGylated version of mechano growth factor with an extended half-life, used in research for muscle repair and recovery via systemic or near-injury dosing.
mechanism: Like MGF, it activates dormant muscle satellite cells through receptor binding and MAPK/ERK signaling, increasing their proliferation, fusion capacity and protein synthesis. PEGylation slows degradation, so a given dose circulates and acts for far longer than native MGF, allowing less frequent injection.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com, 100-200 mcg twice weekly (conservative) up to 200-400 mcg 2-3x weekly, given subcutaneously or intramuscularly near the target muscle, with post-workout timing.
cycle: Run in multi-week blocks (benefits reported through weeks 4-8 and persisting several weeks post-cycle); breaks taken between cycles.
sideEffects: Injection site soreness and mild fatigue; discontinue for unusual growths/lumps, persistent headaches or vision changes, or hypoglycemia. Watch for water retention and joint pain if combined with GH. Contraindicated with cancer history, uncontrolled diabetes, and pregnancy; not approved for human use.
comparisons: PEG-MGF trades MGF's pinpoint localized action for convenience — far fewer injections at the cost of less site-specificity. Versus IGF-1 LR3 it works on muscle stem cells rather than producing broad systemic IGF activity, and it is often stacked with healing peptides like BPC-157 and TB-500.

name: BPC-157
summary: A synthetic 15-amino-acid peptide derived from a gastric protein, widely researched for tendon, ligament, muscle and gastrointestinal tissue repair.
mechanism: BPC-157 promotes angiogenesis (partly via VEGF), enhances collagen synthesis and fibroblast activity, modulates growth factor expression, and modulates nitric oxide pathways for vascular and cytoprotective effects. It accelerates healing of connective tissue, protects gut mucosa, and reduces inflammation, working both locally and systemically. It is unusually stable in gastric juice, supporting oral use for gut-focused research.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com and peptidedosingprotocols.com research-planning ranges: ~250 mcg once or twice daily for general/maintenance use, up to ~500 mcg twice daily for acute injury, typically subcutaneous near the injury site (oral/capsule also used for gut work). Direct human dose-finding data is minimal.
cycle: Standard 4-6 weeks (review at week 4); extended 6-8 weeks or longer 8-12 weeks for slow-remodeling tissue, with 2-4 week off periods between cycles.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated in preclinical work; possible mild injection site reaction. Theoretical caution with anticoagulants (angiogenesis) and with cancer history. Not FDA-approved; evidence is mostly preclinical, so treat protocols as research-context planning, not medical advice.
comparisons: BPC-157 is the most-discussed healing peptide and is commonly paired with TB-500 in the 'Wolverine stack' — BPC-157 leans toward angiogenesis, collagen and gut protection while TB-500 drives cell migration and systemic repair. It uniquely offers an oral route for gut indications, unlike TB-500.

name: TB-500
summary: A synthetic peptide based on the actin-binding region of thymosin beta-4, researched for systemic tissue repair of muscle, tendon, ligament and wounds.
mechanism: It acts as an actin-sequestering peptide, regulating cell migration and proliferation, promoting angiogenesis, reducing inflammation and supporting stem-cell differentiation for broad regenerative effects. Most published human clinical work has actually used full-length thymosin beta-4 rather than the TB-500 fragment, so equivalence is assumed, not proven.
dosing: Per peptidedosingprotocols.com research-context planning (community-derived, not RCT-validated): a loading phase of ~2-2.5 mg subcutaneous twice weekly (~4-5 mg/week) for weeks 1-6, then a maintenance phase of ~2-2.5 mg once weekly for weeks 7-12; reconstitute 10 mg in 2 mL BAC water (5 mg/mL).
cycle: Common research-context cycles run 4-12 weeks (load 4-6 weeks then maintain), followed by a 4-8 week off period; no human cycling RCT exists.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated in available data; mild injection site reactions, possible fatigue. Not FDA-approved, sold for research only, and on the WADA Prohibited List; treat all dose and cycle numbers as research-context planning, not treatment guidance.
comparisons: TB-500 is the more systemic counterpart to BPC-157 and the two are routinely stacked; BPC-157 favors localized angiogenesis and gut protection while TB-500 favors cell migration and whole-body repair. Unlike BPC-157 it has no practical oral route and is dosed far less frequently.

name: BPC-157 + TB-500 Blend
summary: A fixed combination of BPC-157 and TB-500 (the 'Wolverine stack'), used in research for synergistic soft-tissue, joint and wound healing.
mechanism: BPC-157 increases actin production, modulates nitric oxide and promotes angiogenesis and collagen synthesis; TB-500 sequesters actin to drive cell migration and reduce inflammation. Together they aim to recruit fibroblasts and immune cells to injury sites through complementary pathways; one cited study reported ~87.5% improvement in knee-pain patients with a BPC-157/Tβ4 combination.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: BPC-157 ~250 mcg twice daily plus TB-500 ~2 mg twice weekly for general recovery, up to BPC-157 ~500 mcg twice daily plus TB-500 ~2.5 mg twice weekly near the injury site for intensive recovery; maintenance ~250 mcg BPC-157 daily plus ~2 mg TB-500 weekly. Reconstitute each peptide separately and do not mix in one syringe unless a pre-made blend.
cycle: Typically 4-8 weeks with benefits reported building through week 8 and persisting afterward; an off period before repeating, mirroring the individual peptides' cycles.
sideEffects: Mild injection site reactions; monitor for bleeding if used with anticoagulants and avoid with cancer history (angiogenesis concern) or alongside chemotherapy. Neither component is FDA-approved and TB-500 is WADA-banned — research-context use only.
comparisons: Compared to either peptide alone, the blend trades dosing flexibility for convenience and claimed additive healing; some practitioners prefer separate vials so BPC-157 (daily) and TB-500 (weekly) can be timed and titrated independently. Within Healing & Recovery it is the default 'do everything' combo versus single-purpose agents like KPV (anti-inflammatory) or GHK-Cu (skin/collagen).

name: ARA-290
summary: An engineered 11-amino-acid peptide derived from erythropoietin (cibinetide) that delivers tissue-protective and nerve-regenerative effects without stimulating red blood cells; holds FDA Orphan Drug status.
mechanism: ARA-290 activates the Innate Repair Receptor — the EPO receptor / beta-common receptor complex — triggering tissue-protective and anti-inflammatory signaling (reducing TNF-alpha, IL-6, IL-12) and promoting peripheral nerve fiber regeneration and angiogenesis via VEGF, without the erythropoietic effect of EPO so there is no polycythemia or thrombotic risk.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com (reflecting Phase 2 trial dosing): typically 4 mg once daily subcutaneously for neuropathy, with a studied range of 1-8 mg daily SubQ; an acute-intervention pattern of 2 mg three times weekly IV is also noted.
cycle: Trial regimens ran ~28 days continuously; benefits reported peaking by weeks 4-6 and persisting for months via a 'molecular switch' effect, with re-dosing courses as needed.
sideEffects: Excellent safety profile in clinical trials with no serious drug-related events; light-sensitive formulation. Contraindicated with recent anti-TNF biologics (6-month washout), EPO use (2-month washout), pregnancy, and BMI over 34.
comparisons: ARA-290 is the neuropathy/nerve-repair specialist of the healing category — distinct from BPC-157 and TB-500 (broad soft-tissue repair) and from KPV/LL-37 (anti-inflammatory/antimicrobial). It is essentially a safer, non-erythropoietic alternative to EPO for tissue protection.

name: KPV
summary: A tripeptide (lysine-proline-valine) fragment of alpha-MSH with anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity but no pigmentation effect, researched for gut, autoimmune and skin inflammation.
mechanism: KPV enters cells and inhibits inflammatory signaling at the nuclear level, particularly NF-kB, lowering pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) without the immunosuppression of steroids. It uses PepT1 transporters — concentrated in inflamed gut tissue — which is why it can work orally, and it does not engage melanocortin receptors so there is no tanning effect.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com and peptidedosingprotocols.com: 200-300 mcg once daily for general anti-inflammatory use, ~250 mcg twice daily during active inflammation, up to 500 mcg daily (or 500 mcg twice daily short-term) for autoimmune/acute flares; routes include subcutaneous, oral, and topical. SubQ protocols often start at 200 mcg/day and titrate ~100 mcg/week to a 400-500 mcg maintenance window.
cycle: Commonly run 8-12 weeks (e.g., initiation weeks 1-2, titration weeks 3-4, maintenance weeks 5-12); acute flares dosed higher for ~1 week then reduced.
sideEffects: Minimal to none reported; does not cause immunosuppression or pigmentation. Discontinue for signs of infection (rare), severe injection site reactions, paradoxical inflammation, or allergy. Limited data in pregnancy/breastfeeding; not FDA-approved for any indication.
comparisons: KPV is the targeted anti-inflammatory of the healing category — narrower than the structural-repair focus of BPC-157/TB-500 and, unlike Melanotan peptides it shares an alpha-MSH lineage with, it carries no tanning or melanocortin side effects. It is often added to gut-healing stacks alongside BPC-157.

name: VIP
summary: A 28-amino-acid neuropeptide of the glucagon/secretin family with potent vasodilatory, anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects, researched for pulmonary hypertension, CIRS/chronic inflammation, and autoimmune conditions.
mechanism: VIP binds VPAC1 and VPAC2 G-protein-coupled receptors, activating adenylyl cyclase and raising cAMP/PKA, which phosphorylates CREB and other transcription factors. It produces nitric-oxide-mediated vasodilation, bronchodilation, smooth-muscle relaxation, anti-inflammatory Th1/Th2 rebalancing, glucose-dependent insulin secretion, and gut-barrier regulation. Its extremely short ~1-2 minute plasma half-life limits bioavailability.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: ~50-100 mcg once or twice daily (general use) up to 100-200 mcg per dose (research protocols), given subcutaneously, intravenously, or intranasally; must be used promptly after reconstitution due to instability.
cycle: No fixed cycle; given on an ongoing/regular basis with cumulative effects on inflammation over days to weeks (frequent dosing required given the short half-life).
sideEffects: Vasodilation (flushing, warmth), hypotension, increased heart rate, headache, possible diarrhea. Discontinue for severe hypotension, arrhythmia, severe diarrhea, or allergic reaction. Contraindicated with severe hypotension, VIPoma, severe cardiac conditions, and pregnancy.
comparisons: Within Healing & Recovery, VIP is a systemic immunomodulator/vasodilator rather than a structural-repair peptide like BPC-157 or TB-500; it overlaps with KPV on anti-inflammatory action but adds cardiopulmonary effects. Its impractically short half-life is its main limitation versus more stable analogs.

name: LL-37
summary: The human cathelicidin antimicrobial peptide, a 37-amino-acid cationic peptide from hCAP18, researched for chronic wound healing and broad-spectrum antimicrobial defense including drug-resistant bacteria.
mechanism: LL-37 disrupts microbial membranes for direct broad-spectrum killing of bacteria, viruses and fungi (including MRSA biofilms), and promotes wound healing by enhancing keratinocyte migration, angiogenesis and collagen synthesis while modulating innate immune responses. Used topically it has minimal systemic absorption.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: topical 0.5 mg/mL (standard) to 1.6 mg/mL (intensive) gel/cream applied to the wound daily to twice weekly; injectable use ~100-150 mcg once daily subcutaneously.
cycle: Typically 2-8 weeks depending on wound-healing progress, adjusted by clinical response.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; mild injection site reactions or local irritation. Discontinue for spreading inflammation, allergic reaction, persistent flu-like symptoms, signs of wound infection, or worsening healing. Contraindicated with peptide hypersensitivity and non-sterile wound environments; pre-mixed liquid formulations are unstable. Not an approved drug.
comparisons: LL-37 is the antimicrobial/wound-bed specialist of the healing category — it pairs with BPC-157 and TB-500 (which drive tissue regeneration) and synergizes with conventional antibiotics, whereas KPV and ARA-290 act mainly on inflammation and nerve repair rather than infection control.

name: B7-33
summary: A single-chain peptide analog of the relaxin-2 B-chain that selectively activates the relaxin receptor RXFP1, researched for anti-fibrotic, vasodilatory and cardioprotective effects.
mechanism: B7-33 selectively activates RXFP1, inhibiting fibroblast-to-myofibroblast differentiation and collagen deposition, increasing matrix-metalloproteinase activity to remodel extracellular matrix, enhancing nitric-oxide-mediated vasodilation, and reducing inflammatory cytokines. It shows biased agonism — preferentially engaging pERK1/2 while activating cAMP less than native relaxin-2 — and its simple single-chain design replaces relaxin-2's complex two-chain disulfide structure.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com (extrapolated entirely from preclinical animal data, never validated in humans): ~100-250 mcg once daily subcutaneously for anti-fibrotic/cardiovascular support.
cycle: No established human cycle; preclinical timelines show vasodilatory onset in weeks 1-2 and measurable anti-fibrotic changes by weeks 4-8, so multi-week courses are implied.
sideEffects: Injection site reactions and possible transient hypotension (dizziness, lightheadedness). No human safety data exists — all protocols are preclinical extrapolations; teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy, in pre-existing hypotension, and with potent antihypertensives without medical supervision. Treat as experimental.
comparisons: B7-33 is the niche anti-fibrotic of the healing category, acting through the relaxin/RXFP1 axis rather than the angiogenesis and cell-migration pathways of BPC-157 and TB-500; it is far less characterized than those peptides, with no human data, and is mainly of interest for organ fibrosis and cardiac remodeling.

name: GLOW70 (BBG70)
summary: A fixed-ratio three-peptide blend of BPC-157, TB-500 and GHK-Cu (typically ~10 mg BPC-157 / ~10 mg TB-500 / ~50 mg GHK-Cu per vial) marketed for skin rejuvenation and combined tissue regeneration.
mechanism: Each component contributes a distinct action: BPC-157 provides angiogenesis, collagen synthesis and anti-inflammatory effects; TB-500 sequesters actin to drive cell migration and reduce inflammation; GHK-Cu, a copper tripeptide, modulates thousands of genes to stimulate collagen, elastin and glycosaminoglycan synthesis and tissue remodeling. The blend aims for additive regenerative and cosmetic effects, though no study has examined the three together.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: dose per the formulation's instructions (one common label is ~10 mg BPC-157, ~10 mg TB-500, ~50 mg GHK-Cu per dose), given once daily subcutaneously to the abdomen, outer thigh, or upper arm.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks, with visible skin changes reported from weeks 2-4 and peak effects by weeks 8-12; a break before repeating.
sideEffects: Injection site reactions and temporary blue-green discoloration from GHK-Cu; main concern is fixed ratios overdelivering one component — especially copper, where 50 mg of GHK-Cu may far exceed the 1-3 mg injectable range, risking copper toxicity (nausea, jaundice). Do not combine with standalone GHK-Cu or BPC-157. Experimental combination — no peer-reviewed studies of the blend; not an approved product.
comparisons: GLOW70 trades the dosing precision of buying BPC-157, TB-500 and GHK-Cu separately for one-injection convenience; for cosmetic skin goals it overlaps GHK-Cu's territory while adding the soft-tissue repair of the Wolverine-stack peptides, but its fixed copper load makes individual peptides the safer choice for serious users.

name: GLOW50
summary: GLOW50 is a compounded three-peptide regenerative blend combining BPC-157, TB-500 and GHK-Cu, marketed for accelerated soft-tissue healing, recovery and skin quality.
mechanism: BPC-157 upregulates growth factors and angiogenesis to promote tendon, ligament and gut repair. TB-500 (a thymosin beta-4 fragment) promotes actin-driven cell migration, blood vessel formation and reduced inflammation. GHK-Cu, a copper tripeptide, modulates gene expression toward tissue remodeling and collagen synthesis. The blend is positioned as multi-pathway healing in a single injection, though the specific combination has no clinical trials.
dosing: No source-backed protocol exists for this exact ratio; compounded GLOW-type blends are typically dosed subcutaneously at roughly 200-500 mcg total blend once daily, mirroring the related GLOW70 protocol.
cycle: Commonly run 4-6 weeks on, then 2-4 weeks off.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; main reports are injection-site reactions and variable individual response. No long-term human safety data for the combination; copper accumulation is a theoretical concern with extended GHK-Cu use. Experimental compounded product, not approved for human use.
comparisons: GLOW50 is essentially a lower-strength GLOW/GLOW70-style blend; it overlaps heavily with the BPC-157 + TB-500 blend (Wolverine stack) but adds GHK-Cu for skin and remodeling effects. KLOW is the same family plus KPV for stronger anti-inflammatory action. Choosing a blend trades dosing flexibility of single peptides for single-injection convenience.

name: KLOW (BBKG80)
summary: KLOW is a fixed-ratio four-peptide regenerative blend (GHK-Cu, TB-500, BPC-157, KPV) that builds on the GLOW protocol by adding KPV for enhanced anti-inflammatory action; a standard vial holds 80 mg (50 mg GHK-Cu, 10 mg TB-500, 10 mg BPC-157, 10 mg KPV).
mechanism: Each component contributes a distinct pathway: GHK-Cu modulates gene expression for tissue repair and collagen, TB-500 enhances cellular migration and wound healing, BPC-157 upregulates growth factors for tissue regeneration, and KPV inhibits the NF-kB inflammatory pathway. The combination is proposed to give synergistic multi-pathway repair with better injection-site tolerability than GLOW.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 200-500 mcg total blend subcutaneously once daily (conservative start ~200 mcg in the evening), rotating between abdomen, thighs and upper arms.
cycle: 4-6 weeks on, 2-4 weeks off between cycles.
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions are common even with KPV; individual responses vary widely. Discontinue for severe site reactions, infection or allergic reactions. The specific four-peptide formulation has no clinical trials despite individual component research; combining with GLOW causes double-dosing, and components affect GH and clotting pathways so anticoagulant or GH-therapy users need supervision.
comparisons: KLOW is GLOW (GHK-Cu + TB-500 + BPC-157) plus KPV, so it offers the strongest anti-inflammatory profile of the healing-blend family and is claimed to sting less than GLOW. Versus the BPC-157 + TB-500 blend it adds copper-peptide skin benefits and KPV; versus running the four peptides separately you lose dose-tuning flexibility but gain a single daily shot.

name: AOD-9604
summary: AOD-9604 is a synthetic ~16-amino-acid C-terminal fragment of human growth hormone (positions 177-191 with an added tyrosine) studied as a fat-loss agent that promotes lipolysis without the muscle, blood-sugar or IGF-1 effects of full GH.
mechanism: It stimulates lipolysis and inhibits lipogenesis, reportedly via upregulation of beta-3 adrenergic receptor activity in adipose tissue, without binding the GH receptor. It does not raise IGF-1, does not impair glucose metabolism and does not induce insulin resistance. Separate animal work has also explored cartilage repair, especially when combined with hyaluronic acid in osteoarthritis models.
dosing: Per the sources: subcutaneous injection ~250-500 mcg once daily in the morning on an empty stomach (peptide-db.com lists 250-300 mcg standard, 400-500 mcg enhanced); peptidedosingprotocols.com summarizes published trial arms as roughly 300 mcg/day for the first 4 weeks then 500 mcg/day, noting no published human PK study has characterized the subcutaneous route.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks (Phase 2 trial arms ran 12-24 weeks); community planning often uses 8-16 weeks with review every 4 weeks.
sideEffects: Generally mild; higher oral/IV doses (around 1 mg/day) were linked to more headache, diarrhea and GI effects. Not FDA-approved for any therapeutic use and listed on the WADA Prohibited List; FDA reviewed it against compounding inclusion in December 2024. No specific lab monitoring is mandated, but glucose and lipids are reasonable to track.
comparisons: AOD-9604 is the stabilized, drug-development version of HGH Fragment 176-191 and is marketed as a metabolically safer alternative to full hGH because it does not raise IGF-1 or affect glucose. It is far weaker for weight loss than GLP-1 drugs like semaglutide or tirzepatide and Phase 2b trials failed their primary endpoint, so it is best viewed as a mild lipolytic adjunct rather than a primary fat-loss therapy.

name: Adipotide
summary: Adipotide (Prohibitin-TP01) is an experimental chimeric peptidomimetic that targets the blood supply of white fat to trigger fat loss; development was halted after Phase 1 due to kidney toxicity.
mechanism: A CKGGRAKDC homing ligand binds prohibitin/annexin A2 on white adipose tissue endothelium; the linked pro-apoptotic D-(KLAKLAK)2 motif is internalized and disrupts mitochondrial membranes, causing localized endothelial apoptosis, capillary regression and adipocyte death. This peripheral, non-CNS mechanism produces fat loss without primarily suppressing appetite.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: research replication of the obese-primate work used ~0.43 mg/kg subcutaneously once daily, with dose-finding tiers of roughly 0.10-0.75 mg/kg/day; there is no validated human dose.
cycle: Studies used short courses of about 28 days; no established safe human cycle exists.
sideEffects: SERIOUS RISK: Adipotide caused dose-dependent proximal renal tubule injury (nephrotoxicity) in primate trials, which ended its clinical development; the map flags it as high-risk. Reported effects include creatinine elevation, electrolyte shifts and dehydration; partial fat rebound occurs after discontinuation. It is not approved for human use and should be regarded as experimental and potentially dangerous; avoid combining with any other nephrotoxic agents and monitor renal function, electrolytes and volume status.
comparisons: Unlike GLP-1 drugs (semaglutide, tirzepatide) or lipolytics like AOD-9604 that act on appetite or fat metabolism, adipotide physically destroys fat-tissue vasculature, giving rapid fat loss in animals but at an unacceptable kidney-safety cost. Within the fat-loss category it is the highest-risk, least clinically viable option and has no current legitimate human use.

name: 5-Amino-1MQ
summary: 5-Amino-1MQ is a small-molecule (not a true peptide) NNMT inhibitor used in metabolic and longevity protocols to raise cellular NAD+ and improve mitochondrial function, with claimed effects on body composition and insulin sensitivity.
mechanism: It inhibits the enzyme nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT), reducing methylation/degradation of nicotinamide and thereby raising cellular NAD+ levels. Higher NAD+ supports sirtuin activity, mitochondrial biogenesis and efficiency, DNA repair, and metabolic flexibility, which in preclinical models translates to increased metabolic rate, better fat oxidation and improved insulin sensitivity.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: most commonly oral, 25 mg (conservative) up to 100 mg once daily with food (50-75 mg typical maintenance, or 50 mg twice daily split); an injectable subcutaneous route of roughly 150-500 mcg once daily is also described.
cycle: Typically 8-12 weeks of continuous daily use.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; mild GI upset is the main issue and is reduced by taking it with food. Human safety data are limited (preclinical-stage compound). Use caution alongside metformin or other metabolic agents and with blood thinners given sparse interaction data; reasonable to monitor glucose and metabolic markers.
comparisons: 5-Amino-1MQ is positioned as a convenient oral NAD+-boosting metabolic tool alongside NMN/NR precursors and mitochondrial peptides like MOTS-c and SS-31, but it works by preventing NAD+ breakdown rather than supplying substrate. It is far milder than GLP-1 drugs for weight loss and should be seen as a metabolic-optimization adjunct rather than a standalone fat-loss therapy.

name: SLU-PP-332
summary: SLU-PP-332 is a synthetic small-molecule pan-ERR (estrogen-related receptor) agonist, often called an exercise mimetic, that activates the aerobic gene program in muscle and other tissues; it is preclinical-only with no human trial data.
mechanism: It binds and activates ERR-alpha (most potently), ERR-beta and ERR-gamma, nuclear receptors that sit upstream of mitochondrial biogenesis, oxidative phosphorylation, the TCA cycle and fatty acid beta-oxidation. Working with the coactivator PGC-1-alpha, it increases mitochondrial density (about 1.8-fold in models), boosts resting energy expenditure and fat oxidation, improves insulin sensitivity and reduces hepatic steatosis, mimicking endurance-training adaptations without exercise.
dosing: Per the sources: no human dose is established. Published mouse studies used ~25-50 mg/kg via intraperitoneal injection (commonly twice daily, or a single dose ~1 hour pre-exercise); research-grade product is also sold as 50 mg oral tablets and 5-10 mg injectable vials, with community oral protocols typically once daily but never clinically validated.
cycle: No human protocols exist; animal extended-treatment studies ran roughly 4-8 weeks of twice-daily dosing.
sideEffects: Not tested in humans, so the safety profile is unknown; it is not FDA-approved and has no clinical program as of 2026. Theoretical concerns follow from broad ERR activation across heart, muscle, liver and brown fat; treat as experimental and unvalidated.
comparisons: SLU-PP-332 is in the exercise-mimetic class alongside AICAR (AMPK activator) and overlaps conceptually with mitochondrial peptides like MOTS-c and AOD-9604, but it acts at the transcriptional level via ERR receptors rather than on AMPK or fat-mobilization directly. It shows the most striking preclinical fat-loss and endurance data of that group yet has the least human evidence, so it ranks high on promise and high on uncertainty.

name: AICAR
summary: AICAR (acadesine) is a cell-permeable nucleoside analog that activates AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK); originally studied for cardiac ischemia protection, it gained interest as an exercise mimetic for its metabolic effects.
mechanism: Inside cells AICAR is phosphorylated to ZMP, an AMP mimetic that activates AMPK, the master regulator of cellular energy homeostasis. This switches on exercise-like metabolic pathways: increased insulin-independent glucose uptake in some tissues, enhanced fatty acid oxidation, and over time stimulation of mitochondrial biogenesis. Animal studies showed increased running endurance, but human data are limited.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: subcutaneous injection once daily, typically 1-3 mg/day standard, titrating from 1 mg up to 3 mg over about 4 weeks, with advanced use of 3-5 mg/day for 8-12 weeks.
cycle: Roughly 8-12 weeks.
sideEffects: Reported issues include injection-site reactions, mild fatigue during adaptation and possible hypoglycemia; discontinue for severe hypoglycemia symptoms or signs of lactic acidosis. Human safety data are limited and it is on the WADA Prohibited List; combining with metformin or other AMPK-active agents may be additive on glucose, so monitor blood sugar.
comparisons: AICAR is the prototypical AMPK-driven exercise mimetic; SLU-PP-332 reaches similar endpoints through ERR transcription factors and SR-9009 through Rev-ErbA, and these are often discussed as a complementary trio. Compared with proven fat-loss drugs it is weak and poorly studied in humans, making it a niche, experimental metabolic agent rather than a mainstream fat-loss tool.

name: L-Carnitine (injectable)
summary: Injectable L-carnitine (levocarnitine) is an FDA-approved amino-acid derivative that shuttles long-chain fatty acids into mitochondria for energy; the injectable form bypasses the poor absorption of oral supplements and is used for fat metabolism, recovery and clinical carnitine deficiency.
mechanism: L-carnitine acts as a carrier that transports long-chain fatty acids across the inner mitochondrial membrane via the carnitine palmitoyltransferase (CPT) shuttle, the rate-limiting step in fatty acid beta-oxidation. Greater fatty acid delivery supports ATP generation from fat, especially during exercise, and may aid recovery and glycogen sparing.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: subcutaneous or intramuscular injection, typically 50-100 mg/day (standard) up to 100-200 mg/day (advanced) once daily, or 100-200 mg taken 30-60 minutes pre-workout; injectable bioavailability greatly exceeds the ~15-20% of oral.
cycle: About 8-16 weeks, and it can be used continuously.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; possible injection-site reactions, mild nausea and a fishy body odor at higher doses. May interact with thyroid hormones and potentiate anticoagulants such as warfarin, so monitor thyroid levels and INR if relevant; it is the standard FDA-approved treatment for primary and secondary carnitine deficiency.
comparisons: Unlike experimental lipolytics (AOD-9604, adipotide) or exercise mimetics (AICAR, SLU-PP-332), injectable L-carnitine is a well-established, FDA-approved compound with a benign safety profile, but its fat-loss effect is modest and largely dependent on training and a caloric deficit. It is frequently a component of lipotropic blends like Lipo-C and Super Shred rather than a powerful standalone agent.

name: Lipo-B (LC120)
summary: Lipo-B is a compounded lipotropic injection combining the MIC complex (methionine, inositol, choline) with vitamin B12 (typically cyanocobalamin), marketed as a fat-metabolism and energy support adjunct to diet and exercise.
mechanism: Methionine and choline are lipotropic agents that help mobilize and metabolize fat in the liver and support phospholipid and bile production; inositol assists fat transport and insulin signaling; vitamin B12 supports red blood cell formation, nerve function and energy metabolism, and is often the perceived energizing component. Together they are claimed to support hepatic fat clearance, though robust controlled weight-loss evidence is lacking.
dosing: No clinical-trial dosing exists for the proprietary blend; in practice Lipo-B is given as a weekly intramuscular injection (commonly 1 mL into the deltoid or gluteal muscle), sometimes up to twice weekly, alongside a reduced-calorie diet.
cycle: Typically run weekly on an ongoing basis while pursuing weight loss, with no formal on/off requirement; many clinics use 8-12 week courses then reassess.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; possible injection-site soreness, transient nausea, urinary urgency and mild GI upset; B12 injections rarely cause flushing or allergic reaction. It is a compounded product without FDA approval for weight loss and lacks strong efficacy evidence; no specific lab monitoring is required, though checking B12 status is reasonable with chronic use.
comparisons: Lipo-B is essentially MIC plus B12, so it sits between a plain MIC injection and the more loaded blends like Lipo-C (adds L-carnitine) or Super Shred (multi-ingredient); all are mild metabolic/energy adjuncts and none approach the efficacy of GLP-1 drugs. Choice among the lipotropic blends comes down to which add-ons (carnitine, extra B vitamins, amino acids) a clinic includes.

name: Lipo-C (LC526)
summary: Lipo-C is a compounded lipotropic injection pairing the MIC complex (methionine, inositol, choline) with L-carnitine, and usually B vitamins, intended to support fat metabolism, fat transport into mitochondria and exercise energy.
mechanism: Methionine and choline act as lipotropics aiding hepatic fat mobilization and phospholipid/bile synthesis; inositol supports fat transport and insulin signaling; L-carnitine shuttles long-chain fatty acids into mitochondria for beta-oxidation; added B vitamins (often B12 and B-complex) support energy metabolism. The combination is marketed to enhance fat utilization more than MIC alone, though controlled efficacy data are limited.
dosing: No trial-based dosing for the proprietary blend; clinically administered as an intramuscular injection (typically ~1 mL) once or twice weekly alongside a calorie-controlled diet and exercise.
cycle: Generally given weekly on an ongoing basis while losing weight; no mandatory on/off cycle, with periodic reassessment (often every 8-12 weeks).
sideEffects: Usually mild: injection-site discomfort, transient nausea, urinary urgency, mild GI upset; rare flushing or allergic reactions from the B12/B-complex component. Compounded, not FDA-approved for weight loss, with weak efficacy evidence; no specific monitoring labs are required.
comparisons: Lipo-C is the L-carnitine-enriched member of the lipotropic family, a step up in ingredients from Lipo-B (MIC + B12) and roughly comparable to other carnitine-containing blends like Super Shred. As with all MIC injections it is a modest adjunct to lifestyle change and far less effective than pharmacologic weight-loss agents.

name: Super Shred (LC553)
summary: Super Shred is a compounded multi-ingredient lipotropic injection built on the MIC complex (methionine, inositol, choline) with added L-carnitine, B vitamins and often supporting amino acids, marketed as an enhanced fat-loss and energy formula.
mechanism: It combines the standard lipotropic actions of methionine and choline (hepatic fat mobilization, phospholipid and bile synthesis) and inositol (fat transport, insulin signaling) with L-carnitine for mitochondrial fatty acid uptake and B vitamins for energy metabolism; some versions add amino acids aimed at supporting metabolic rate. The premise is broader metabolic support than simpler MIC blends, but evidence for meaningful added fat loss is weak.
dosing: No clinical-trial dosing for the proprietary mix; typically given as an intramuscular injection (around 1 mL) once or twice weekly with a reduced-calorie diet and exercise.
cycle: Usually weekly on an ongoing basis during a weight-loss phase; no formal on/off cycle, reassessed periodically.
