sideEffects: Generally mild: injection-site soreness, transient nausea, urinary urgency, GI upset; occasional flushing or allergic reaction from the vitamin components. Compounded and not FDA-approved for weight loss; efficacy data are limited and it should be seen as an adjunct, not a primary therapy. No mandatory monitoring labs.
comparisons: Super Shred is the most loaded of the common lipotropic blends, adding carnitine, extra B vitamins and amino acids on top of MIC, where Lipo-B is MIC + B12 and Lipo-C is MIC + carnitine. Despite the longer ingredient list its real-world effect remains modest and far below GLP-1 medications; the differences between these blends are largely formulation marketing.

name: MIC+B (LC216)
summary: MIC+B is the classic lipotropic injection: methionine, inositol and choline (the MIC complex) combined with vitamin B12, used as a metabolic and energy support adjunct during weight-loss programs.
mechanism: Methionine and choline are lipotropic compounds that assist mobilization and breakdown of fat in the liver and support phospholipid and bile synthesis; inositol aids intracellular fat transport and insulin signaling; vitamin B12 supports red cell production, neurological function and energy metabolism and is the component most associated with the perceived energy boost. The blend is positioned to help the liver process fat, though high-quality weight-loss efficacy data are lacking.
dosing: No trial-based dosing for the proprietary blend; typically administered as a weekly intramuscular injection (commonly ~1 mL in the deltoid or gluteal muscle), occasionally twice weekly, alongside a calorie-restricted diet.
cycle: Generally given weekly on an ongoing basis during a weight-loss phase; no required on/off cycle, with periodic reassessment.
sideEffects: Usually mild: injection-site discomfort, transient nausea, urinary urgency, mild GI upset; rare flushing or allergic reaction to the B12 component. It is a compounded product not FDA-approved for weight loss with weak efficacy evidence; no specific monitoring labs are required, though B12 status can be checked with long-term use.
comparisons: MIC+B is the baseline lipotropic injection; Lipo-B is essentially the same MIC + B12 formula, while Lipo-C and Super Shred add L-carnitine and other ingredients. All are mild adjuncts to diet and exercise and none rival pharmacologic weight-loss agents, so MIC+B is the simplest, cheapest entry point in this category.

name: HCG
summary: HCG is a placental glycoprotein hormone that acts as an LH-receptor agonist, FDA-approved for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, cryptorchidism and ovulation induction, and widely used to maintain testicular function during TRT.
mechanism: HCG binds LH receptors on testicular Leydig cells, stimulating intratesticular testosterone and estrogen biosynthesis with a 24-36 hour half-life and peak levels 6-12 hours post-injection. In women it triggers final follicular maturation and ovulation. Because testes also express aromatase, the rise in intratesticular testosterone can elevate estrogen.
dosing: Per peptide-db: 250-500 IU every other day (low TRT adjunct), 500-1000 IU twice weekly (standard TRT adjunct), 1500-2000 IU 2-3x weekly IM for monotherapy or fertility, 5000-10,000 IU single IM/SubQ ovulation trigger, and 1000-1500 IU EOD for 2-3 weeks for PCT; routes SubQ or IM.
cycle: Ongoing alongside TRT, or 3-6 month courses for fertility protocols; pediatric cryptorchidism dosed 2-3x weekly for 3-4 weeks.
sideEffects: Gynecomastia and breast tenderness from increased estrogen, headaches, irritability and mood swings; in fertility use, risk of OHSS. Monitor estradiol, testosterone and (in women) follicle response by ultrasound. Potential anti-HCG antibody formation with long-term use.
comparisons: Within hormonal/fertility agents, HCG directly mimics LH with a long half-life and is simpler to dose than pulsatile gonadorelin, while gonadorelin and kisspeptin act upstream for more physiological HPG-axis restoration. HMG supplies both FSH and LH and is used alongside HCG for spermatogenesis induction.

name: HMG
summary: HMG is a purified gonadotropin preparation from postmenopausal urine containing FSH and LH in roughly a 1:1 ratio, FDA-approved for ovulation induction, IVF stimulation and male infertility.
mechanism: The FSH component stimulates growth and maturation of ovarian follicles (and spermatogenesis in men) while the LH component supports follicular development, steroidogenesis and testicular testosterone production. Highly purified HP-hMG has enhanced FSH-receptor activity with reduced inactive proteins; FSH component half-life is roughly 39-54 hours.
dosing: Per peptide-db: 75-150 IU daily IM or SubQ for ovulation induction, 150-300 IU daily for 7-12 days for IVF stimulation, and 75-150 IU 2-3x weekly IM/SubQ for male fertility; dosing is individualized to follicular response and estradiol levels.
cycle: 7-12 day stimulation phase per IVF/ovulation cycle starting around cycle day 2-3, with HCG trigger when follicles mature; male fertility courses run months.
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions, abdominal discomfort, ovarian enlargement, mood changes and headache; main serious risk is ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS), monitored by ultrasound follicle counts and serum estradiol. The LH content may give a lower OHSS rate than FSH-only protocols.
comparisons: Compared with recombinant FSH, HMG adds LH activity to better mimic natural physiology, is roughly 40-60% cheaper, and may improve fertilization rates with comparable pregnancy outcomes. It is typically paired with an HCG trigger and is interchangeable with recombinant gonadotropins in most settings.

name: Kisspeptin-10
summary: Kisspeptin-10 is a 10-amino-acid fragment of the KISS1 gene product that activates GPR54/KISS1R receptors to drive GnRH release; studied for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, fertility/IVF triggering and sexual function. Not FDA-approved.
mechanism: It binds GPR54/KISS1R receptors on hypothalamic GnRH neurons, triggering pulsatile GnRH release that stimulates pituitary LH/FSH secretion and downstream gonadal steroidogenesis. Half-life is about 4 minutes for KP-10 (about 28-32 minutes for KP-54), and continuous exposure causes receptor desensitization (tachyphylaxis), so it must be pulsed.
dosing: Per peptidedosingprotocols and peptide-db: kisspeptin-10 commonly 50-100 mcg once daily SC (low pulse), 100-200 mcg once daily SC (standard), or 100 mcg twice daily SC; kisspeptin-54 trial doses are weight-based (about 1.6-12.8 nmol/kg single SC bolus for IVF triggering, 6.4 nmol/kg twice weekly for amenorrhea). NEVER daily long-term — desensitization within days.
cycle: Short pulse cycles of 2-4 weeks with 2-4 weeks off (community standard cycle 4-6 weeks on / 4 weeks off); or single diagnostic/IVF-trigger dose.
sideEffects: Minimal acute side effects; occasional injection-site reactions, headache or transient visual disturbance. Avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding and use caution with cardiovascular disease. Daily dosing causes receptor desensitization and loss of effect. Research-use only, not FDA-approved.
comparisons: As an IVF oocyte-maturation trigger, kisspeptin causes far less moderate/severe OHSS than HCG because it stimulates the body's own LH surge rather than replacing it; like gonadorelin it acts upstream of the pituitary but at the kisspeptin receptor rather than the GnRH receptor, and both demand pulsatile dosing unlike the long-acting HCG.

name: Gonadorelin
summary: Gonadorelin is synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), a 10-amino-acid peptide used diagnostically and, via pulsatile dosing, therapeutically to stimulate pituitary LH/FSH release for fertility and TRT support.
mechanism: It agonizes GnRH receptors on the anterior pituitary; when delivered in pulses mimicking natural secretion (every 90-120 min) it triggers LH and FSH release and downstream gonadal steroidogenesis, but continuous exposure paradoxically desensitizes and downregulates the receptor, suppressing gonadotropins. Half-life is only 2-4 minutes.
dosing: Per peptide-db: 100 mcg single SubQ/IV injection for diagnostic testing, 50-100 mcg 2-3x weekly SubQ for fertility support, 5-20 mcg per pulse every 90-120 min via SubQ pump for pulsatile therapy, and 100-200 mcg 2-3x weekly SubQ as a TRT adjunct.
cycle: Varies by use: single-dose for diagnostics, ongoing for fertility therapy, continuous for TRT support, 2-3 week courses for post-cycle therapy.
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions, headache, flushing, nausea and abdominal discomfort. Misjudged dosing frequency (continuous instead of pulsatile) causes receptor desensitization and gonadotropin suppression. Monitor LH/FSH and testosterone response.
comparisons: Compared with HCG for maintaining testicular function on TRT, gonadorelin restores the HPG axis more physiologically by driving endogenous LH/FSH but requires impractical pulsatile dosing, whereas HCG directly mimics LH with a 24-36 hour half-life and is far simpler to administer. Kisspeptin acts one step upstream of gonadorelin.

name: Oxytocin
summary: Oxytocin is a nine-amino-acid neuropeptide hormone, FDA-approved (IV/IM) for labor induction and postpartum hemorrhage, and investigated via intranasal/subcutaneous routes for social cognition, anxiety, PTSD, autism and sexual function.
mechanism: It binds oxytocin receptors on uterine smooth muscle, driving calcium influx, myometrial contraction and prostaglandin release. Centrally it modulates GABAergic, serotonergic and dopaminergic signaling, reduces amygdala reactivity and dampens HPA-axis cortisol output; it also cross-reacts with vasopressin receptors at higher doses. Plasma half-life is 3-6 minutes (about 20 minutes central after intranasal).
dosing: Per sources: obstetric labor induction 0.5-2 mU/min IV titrated; postpartum hemorrhage 10 units IM or 10-40 units in IV solution. Investigational routes (peptide-db / peptidedosingprotocols): intranasal 10-72 IU (about 17-120 mcg), commonly 24 IU once per session 30-45 min before activity or 1-2x daily for chronic protocols; subcutaneous 100-500 mcg daily titrated from 100 mcg.
cycle: Obstetric use is acute/single-event; behavioral protocols run roughly 4-12 weeks of daily dosing, or as-needed (max one dose per 24 hours for sexual-function use).
sideEffects: Mild headache, nasal irritation (intranasal), nausea/vomiting and transient blood-pressure changes; obstetric IV use risks uterine hyperstimulation and fetal distress, and water intoxication (confusion, seizures, edema) can occur. Alcohol suppresses oxytocin effects. FDA-approved only for obstetric use; intranasal/SubQ uses are investigational.
comparisons: The FDA-approved obstetric injection is high-dose IV with immediate onset and tight titration, whereas investigational intranasal/SubQ oxytocin uses much lower doses for neuromodulation with mixed efficacy across indications; for sexual-function research it is sometimes paired with PT-141, which works through a separate melanocortin pathway.

name: PT-141
summary: PT-141 (bremelanotide) is a cyclic melanocortin-receptor agonist, FDA-approved as Vyleesi for premenopausal women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder and used off-label for male erectile dysfunction; it works centrally rather than on blood flow.
mechanism: It selectively activates melanocortin MC3R/MC4R receptors in the CNS (medial preoptic area of the hypothalamus), increasing dopamine release and triggering sexual arousal pathways independent of peripheral vascular mechanisms. Onset is within about 45 minutes with effects lasting 6-12 hours; half-life is about 2.7 hours.
dosing: Per sources: FDA-approved Vyleesi dose 1.75 mg subcutaneously at least 45 minutes before sexual activity, no more than one dose per 24 hours and no more than 8 doses per month; men commonly use 1-2 mg SubQ, with a 0.5 mg test dose for tolerance. Investigational intranasal arms studied 7.5-20 mg; compounded nasal sprays cite about 1-2 mg per actuation.
cycle: Used on-demand before sexual activity rather than in cycles; capped at 1 dose/24 h and 8 doses/month under the Vyleesi label.
sideEffects: Nausea (about 40%), flushing (about 20%), headache (about 11%) and transient blood-pressure elevation; contraindicated with nitrates and cautioned with antihypertensives due to additive hypotension risk, and skin/gum hyperpigmentation can occur with repeated dosing. Pre-treating with an antiemetic is commonly recommended.
comparisons: Unlike PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil/tadalafil) that act peripherally on blood flow, PT-141 acts centrally on desire and arousal and works in PDE5-resistant cases; it shares melanocortin ancestry with Melanotan 2 but is MC3R/MC4R-preferring with far less tanning/mole risk, and it has a cleaner, FDA-approved profile than off-label oxytocin for sexual function.

name: Melanotan 2
summary: Melanotan 2 is a synthetic non-selective alpha-MSH analog used for UV-free tanning and sexual enhancement; it is not approved for human use anywhere and is sold only as a research compound.
mechanism: It binds melanocortin receptors broadly — MC1R drives melanin production for tanning, MC4R affects sexual arousal and appetite, with MC3R/MC5R also engaged — producing several effects (and side effects) at once. Plasma half-life is about 1-2 hours, but pigmentary and behavioral effects last 24-48+ hours because they depend on slower biological changes.
dosing: Per sources: a loading phase of about 100-250 mcg daily SubQ escalating to 500-1000 mcg daily over 4-6 weeks as nausea tolerance allows, then maintenance of 250-1000 mcg 1-2x weekly; a 'pre-tan only' variant starts at 50-75 mcg on tanning days only, increasing 25 mcg every 3 sessions. ED-trial context used about 0.025 mg/kg (roughly 1.75 mg at 70 kg).
cycle: Typical cycles 4-8 weeks of loading then weekly maintenance, with 4-6+ weeks off; many frameworks pause in winter and re-load before sun exposure.
sideEffects: Nausea and facial flushing (worst in the first 1-2 weeks), fatigue, yawning, appetite suppression and spontaneous erections; serious concerns include priapism, rhabdomyolysis at overdose levels, and — most importantly — darkening of existing moles, new naevi and case reports of melanoma, so it is contraindicated with personal/family history of melanoma or many atypical moles. Baseline dermatology skin exam and serial mole photography are essential; not FDA-approved.
comparisons: Within cosmetic/skin agents, Melanotan 2 is the non-selective heavyweight — strong tanning and sexual effects but dose-limiting nausea and the highest mole/melanoma risk — versus MC1R-preferring afamelanotide (Melanotan 1), which has a milder side-effect profile and an FDA-approved implant form, and MC3R/MC4R-preferring PT-141, which is approved for sexual desire with minimal tanning.

name: Melanotan 1
summary: Melanotan 1 (afamelanotide) is an MC1R-preferring alpha-MSH analog, FDA-approved as the SCENESSE subcutaneous implant for erythropoietic protoporphyria and used in research-grade injectable form for tanning and photoprotection.
mechanism: It selectively activates MC1R receptors to stimulate eumelanin synthesis, increasing skin pigmentation and photoprotection; its short (~30 minute) half-life in injectable form requires multiple daily doses for sustained effect, while the approved version is a slow-release implant.
dosing: Per peptide-db: research-grade injectable loading of 0.25-0.5 mg 1-2x daily SubQ for 1-2 weeks, then maintenance of 0.25-0.5 mg 2-3x weekly SubQ; the FDA-approved form is a 16 mg SCENESSE implant. UV exposure cuts the required UV dose roughly in half.
cycle: 2-4 week loading phase then ongoing maintenance as needed (implant dosed periodically per the SCENESSE label).
sideEffects: Mild nausea, facial flushing, reduced appetite and increased mole/freckle pigmentation; mole changes are less common than with Melanotan 2 because of reduced MC3R/MC4R activation, but skin self-examination is still important. Discontinue for severe persistent vomiting or rapidly changing moles. Off-label injectable use is unapproved.
comparisons: Compared with Melanotan 2, MT-1 is MC1R-selective so it gives tanning/photoprotection with a milder side-effect profile and lower mole/melanoma risk, and unlike MT-2 it has an FDA-approved implant form; it lacks the strong sexual/appetite effects of MT-2 and the central sexual-arousal action of PT-141.

name: GHK-Cu
summary: GHK-Cu (glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine copper) is a small copper-binding tripeptide used topically and by subcutaneous injection for skin regeneration, anti-aging and wound healing; the topical (cosmetic Copper Tripeptide-1) route has the strongest human evidence.
mechanism: It penetrates skin, activates fibroblasts, and stimulates collagen and elastin synthesis while modulating matrix metalloproteinases, supports angiogenesis and antioxidant defense, and broadly shifts gene expression toward patterns associated with younger tissue. Half-life is about 0.5-2 hours; plasma levels decline with age.
dosing: Per sources: topical formulations of about 0.5-3% applied once or twice daily for 8-12+ weeks; injectable (research-use, community-derived) about 1-2 mg per day SubQ for 4-8 week cycles, e.g. starting around 1 mg/day. Topical use is the better-evidenced route.
cycle: Topical 8-12+ weeks ongoing; injectable cycles 4-8 weeks with 2-4 week off-periods between cycles.
sideEffects: Topical: mild local redness, tingling or dryness, more common above ~4% and worse when layered with strong retinoids or low-pH acids; injectable: local redness, mild swelling, transient warmth or a faint blue-tinted bruise. Theoretical concerns about copper accumulation and ECM imbalance; avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding, active cancer, Wilson's disease/copper disorders and copper allergy. Not an FDA-approved drug.
comparisons: Within cosmetic/skin peptides, GHK-Cu is the best-studied copper peptide for skin (collagen density, fine lines, laxity) versus AHK-Cu, a related copper tripeptide aimed more at hair follicles; it is the active in the GLOW/KLOW community blends (with BPC-157, TB-500, and in KLOW kisspeptin-10) but those fixed-ratio stacks lack their own trials.

name: AHK-Cu
summary: AHK-Cu (alanyl-histidyl-lysine copper, Copper Tripeptide-3) is a copper tripeptide used in topical formulations primarily to stimulate hair growth via dermal papilla cells, with secondary skin-regeneration effects.
mechanism: It delivers copper to scalp dermal papilla cells, stimulating follicle elongation and cell proliferation, raising the anti-apoptotic Bcl-2/Bax ratio, upregulating VEGF for blood flow, reducing TGF-beta1 (which counters DHT-driven miniaturization) and boosting SOD antioxidant activity; it also activates fibroblasts and collagen synthesis in skin. Plasma half-life is not established.
dosing: Per peptide-db: topical 0.5-1% solution or serum applied once or twice daily to the scalp; combined hair protocols use about 0.3-0.5% AHK-Cu with minoxidil; skin use about 0.3-1% in serum/cream, with formulation pH kept around 5.5-7.0.
cycle: At least 12-24 weeks of consistent use for hair-growth effects, then ongoing for maintenance.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated topically; rare scalp irritation (patch test advised), mild redness on sensitive skin, possible early shedding. Discontinue for persistent irritation, allergic reaction, or signs of copper sensitivity (metallic taste, nausea). Avoid combining with extra oral copper. Cosmetic ingredient, not an FDA-approved drug.
comparisons: AHK-Cu is the hair-focused counterpart to GHK-Cu, which has stronger evidence for skin anti-aging endpoints; in hair-loss stacks it complements minoxidil (vasodilation/potassium channels) and 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (DHT blockade) through a distinct dermal-papilla-stimulating mechanism, and it is more limited than GHK-Cu in documented human data.

name: Glutathione (injectable)
summary: Glutathione is the body's master antioxidant tripeptide (glutamate-cysteine-glycine); injectable forms are used for antioxidant/detox support and, popularly, for skin brightening, with research interest also in neurological and immune health.
mechanism: It is the primary intracellular antioxidant, directly neutralizing reactive oxygen species and serving as a cofactor for glutathione peroxidase, recycling other antioxidants (e.g. regenerating oxidized vitamin C), conjugating heavy metals and xenobiotics for elimination, protecting mitochondria and maintaining redox balance; the cysteine thiol group provides the activity. It also modulates melanin synthesis, the basis of its skin-lightening effect. IV half-life is about 10-14 minutes.
dosing: Per peptide-db: 100-200 mg 2-3x weekly SubQ for general wellness, 200-600 mg 2-3x weekly SubQ or IV for intensive therapy, 600-1000 mg weekly IV for detoxification, and up to 1400 mg 2-3x weekly IV in Parkinson's research; injectable is favored over oral for bioavailability.
cycle: Typically 8-12 week courses.
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions, mild nausea (more with higher doses) and abdominal cramping; IV use has been associated with rare but serious allergic reactions including anaphylaxis (and Stevens-Johnson-type reactions reported with skin-lightening use). Use the reduced (GSH) form, not oxidized (GSSG). Not an FDA-approved drug for these uses.
comparisons: Among cosmetic/skin agents, glutathione is a systemic antioxidant that lightens skin tone via melanin modulation rather than a topical collagen-stimulating peptide like GHK-Cu or a hair peptide like AHK-Cu; it pairs well with vitamin C and is often stacked with NAD+ in longevity protocols, but its skin-lightening efficacy is variable and IV use carries more safety concerns than oral.

name: Hyaluronic Acid (injectable)
summary: Injectable hyaluronic acid (sodium hyaluronate) is a naturally occurring glycosaminoglycan used as a dermal filler for soft-tissue volumizing and wrinkle correction and as an intra-articular viscosupplement for osteoarthritis.
mechanism: HA is a large, highly hydrophilic polysaccharide that binds water to provide tissue volume, hydration and viscoelasticity; cross-linked dermal-filler forms physically lift and contour tissue while stimulating some collagen response, and intra-articular HA restores synovial fluid viscoelasticity, lubricating and cushioning the joint and modestly dampening inflammation. It is gradually degraded by hyaluronidase over months.
dosing: Dermal fillers are injected to effect into the dermis/subcutis (typically a fraction of a mL up to a few mL per treatment area) by a trained injector, with touch-ups as the product resorbs over roughly 6-18 months depending on the product; intra-articular OA viscosupplementation is typically a single injection or a course of 1-5 weekly injections per joint per cycle.
cycle: Not cycled in the peptide sense; dermal-filler results last about 6-18 months before repeat treatment, and intra-articular courses are repeated roughly every 6 months as needed.
sideEffects: Injection-site swelling, bruising, redness, tenderness and lumps/nodules; uncommon but serious risks of dermal filler include vascular occlusion causing skin necrosis or (rarely) blindness, infection, biofilm and delayed inflammatory nodules — hyaluronidase can dissolve HA filler in emergencies. Intra-articular HA can cause transient joint pain or effusion. Generally regarded as low-risk when injected by qualified practitioners.
comparisons: As a filler, HA is the reversible, predictable workhorse versus longer-lasting biostimulatory or permanent fillers, and it is often used as a delivery base for topical copper peptides like GHK-Cu and AHK-Cu; as a joint viscosupplement it offers a slower-onset, longer-duration alternative to intra-articular corticosteroids.

name: SNAP-8
summary: SNAP-8 (acetyl octapeptide-3) is a synthetic octapeptide used topically as a needle-free 'Botox alternative' that softens dynamic expression lines around the eyes and forehead.
mechanism: It mimics the N-terminal of the SNAP-25 protein and competitively inhibits formation of the SNARE complex, which is required for vesicle docking and acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction. Reduced acetylcholine release means less repetitive micro-contraction of facial muscles, so dynamic wrinkles deepen more slowly. Reported inhibition efficiency is around 43% at therapeutic concentrations, with clinical data showing up to 63% reduction in wrinkle depth over 28 days.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: topical use at 3-10% concentration in a serum or cream applied twice daily (morning and evening); injectable form roughly 0.33-1 mg. Eye-area and preventive use at the lower 3-5% end, intensive wrinkle treatment at 5-10%.
cycle: Continuous daily use; effects are maintained only while applied, so there is no on/off cycling for the topical product.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated topically; possible mild local irritation, redness, or dryness at the application site. No systemic effects expected from cosmetic use. It is a cosmetic-grade ingredient, not an approved drug; injectable use is experimental and not validated.
comparisons: Within topical anti-wrinkle peptides, SNAP-8 is an extended, slightly more potent analog of argireline (acetyl hexapeptide-8) and targets the same neuromuscular pathway, whereas Matrixyl works by signalling collagen synthesis rather than relaxing muscle. SNAP-8 is best for dynamic expression lines; copper peptides like GHK-Cu and Matrixyl are better for overall firmness, texture, and static lines, and the two mechanisms are often combined in one routine.

name: Matrixyl
summary: Matrixyl is a palmitoylated matrikine peptide (palmitoyl pentapeptide-4, often blended as Matrixyl 3000) used topically to stimulate collagen and reduce fine lines and skin laxity.
mechanism: The pentapeptide fragment is a matrikine that signals fibroblasts to upregulate synthesis of collagen I, III and IV, fibronectin, hyaluronic acid and glycosaminoglycans, effectively mimicking the wound-healing repair signal without injury. The palmitoyl tail improves lipophilicity so the peptide penetrates the stratum corneum. Matrixyl 3000 pairs it with palmitoyl tripeptide-1 for a broader matrikine effect, gradually thickening the dermal matrix and smoothing the skin surface.
dosing: No source dump available; compiled. Typical cosmetic use is 3-8% of the branded solution incorporated into a serum or moisturizer, applied once or twice daily.
cycle: Continuous daily use; benefits accrue over 4-12 weeks and are maintained only with ongoing application.
sideEffects: Very low irritation potential; occasional mild redness or stinging in sensitive users. No systemic absorption or monitoring required. This is a cosmetic ingredient, not a drug; effect sizes are modest compared with retinoids.
comparisons: Among topical anti-wrinkle peptides, Matrixyl is a collagen-stimulating signal peptide, unlike SNAP-8 and argireline which are neuromuscular relaxers, and unlike GHK-Cu which combines collagen signalling with copper-driven antioxidant and remodelling effects. It is gentler than retinoids with a smaller effect on photoaging, and is frequently layered with copper peptides and SNAP-8 since the mechanisms are complementary.

name: Lemon Bottle
summary: Lemon Bottle is a Korean injectable lipolysis ('fat dissolving') solution marketed for small localized fat deposits such as the chin, jawline, and stubborn body pockets.
mechanism: It is a cocktail rather than a single peptide; the headline actives are riboflavin (vitamin B2), bromelain, and lecithin/phosphatidylcholine-type emulsifiers. The lecithin component is intended to emulsify and disrupt adipocyte membranes so the released triglycerides are cleared, while riboflavin and bromelain are promoted as supporting metabolism and reducing inflammation. The exact formulation is proprietary and not standardized, and rigorous clinical evidence is limited.
dosing: No source dump available; compiled. Practitioners typically inject small aliquots (on the order of 0.2-0.5 mL per point) across a treatment grid in the target area, repeated every 2-4 weeks for 2-4 sessions; no validated standardized dose exists.
cycle: Course of roughly 2-4 sessions spaced 2-4 weeks apart, then assess; not a continuously cycled product.
sideEffects: Expect swelling, bruising, tenderness, warmth, and firmness at injection sites for days to weeks; risk of nodules, prolonged inflammation, infection, skin necrosis, and uneven fat loss. It is not FDA-approved, formulations are unregulated and have been linked to adverse-event warnings in several countries, so it should be considered experimental and higher-risk than approved deoxycholic-acid products.
comparisons: Compared with the FDA-approved injectable lipolytic deoxycholic acid (Kybella), Lemon Bottle is cheaper and marketed as less painful, but lacks the controlled-trial evidence and regulatory clearance behind it; versus the lipotropic injectables in this category (lipo-B, MIC) it is intended for direct local fat reduction rather than systemic metabolic support, and versus CoolSculpting-type devices it is a chemical rather than physical approach with a less predictable safety profile.

name: Selank
summary: Selank is a synthetic heptapeptide analog of the immune peptide tuftsin (also called TP-7), developed in Russia and studied for anxiety relief with mild focus support and without sedation, tolerance, or withdrawal.
mechanism: It works largely through allosteric modulation of GABA-A receptors plus optimization of serotonin and dopamine signalling, and it inhibits enkephalin degradation, prolonging endogenous opioid/enkephalin tone. It also regulates IL-6 and other cytokines (an echo of tuftsin's immune role) and upregulates hippocampal BDNF (about a 30% increase), which is thought to support stress resilience and neuroprotection.
dosing: Per peptidedosingprotocols.com: subcutaneous 250-500 mcg per day, or intranasal 600-2,700 mcg per day split into 2-3 sprays; pick one route per cycle. peptide-db.com reports 250-500 mcg per dose, 1-2x daily.
cycle: SubQ: 4 weeks on, 4 weeks off (some use a 5-on/2-off weekly pattern); intranasal: 14-21 days on followed by 1-3 weeks off. peptide-db lists 2-8 weeks on.
sideEffects: Generally mild: nasal irritation or congestion with the spray, occasional headache, fatigue, or transient blood-pressure changes; no tolerance or withdrawal reported. Approved in Russia for anxiety disorders/neurasthenia but not FDA-approved, so it is not for unsupervised human use; no specific lab monitoring is established.
comparisons: Within cognitive/mood peptides, Selank is the anxiolytic counterpart to Semax's more stimulating, attention-and-memory profile, and the two are often run together; unlike benzodiazepines it provides calm without sedation, amnesia, or dependence, and unlike SSRIs it has a rapid onset. Its evidence base is largely Russian clinical use rather than Western trials.

name: Semax
summary: Semax is a synthetic heptapeptide (Met-Glu-His-Phe-Pro-Gly-Pro) derived from ACTH fragment 4-10 with a stabilizing Pro-Gly-Pro tail, developed in Russia for stroke recovery and used in research for cognitive enhancement, focus, and neuroprotection.
mechanism: It rapidly raises BDNF and NGF levels and modulates dopaminergic and serotonergic systems; the Pro-Gly-Pro tail slows enzymatic breakdown, extending the functional window of the bare ACTH fragment. Given intranasally it reaches the CNS directly via olfactory epithelium and trigeminal nerve transport, achieving roughly 0.093% brain penetration versus about 0.01% by IV, largely bypassing the blood-brain barrier.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 300-600 mcg per dose (up to 1000 mcg for intensive use), 1-2x daily, morning preferred for the cognitive boost. peptidedosingprotocols.com notes intranasal solution is the most-studied format with daily dosing often split morning/afternoon to avoid late-day stimulation; injectable math assumes 20 mg lyophilized vials reconstituted before subcutaneous use.
cycle: Roughly 2-4 weeks on followed by a break; Russian clinical references describe courses of several weeks rather than continuous indefinite use.
sideEffects: Usually mild: nasal irritation, headache, overstimulation, irritability, or trouble sleeping if dosed late; rare blood-pressure or mood changes. Not FDA-approved (removed from FDA's 503A Category 2 list in April 2026 with a PCAC review scheduled July 2026); not authorized for human use, and no specific monitoring labs are established.
comparisons: Among cognitive peptides, Semax is the prototypical stimulating nootropic, more focus- and memory-oriented than the calming Selank and shorter-acting than Adamax (its adamantane-modified successor with an 8-10 h half-life). It overlaps with Cerebrolysin in raising neurotrophic factors but is a single small peptide self-administered intranasally rather than a clinic-administered IV protein mixture.

name: Cerebrolysin
summary: Cerebrolysin is a standardized neuropeptide preparation of low-molecular-weight peptides and amino acids purified from porcine brain, used clinically in 50+ countries for stroke, traumatic brain injury, vascular dementia, and Alzheimer's disease.
mechanism: Given IV or IM, it delivers a mixture of neurotrophic peptides and amino acids that mimic the actions of endogenous neurotrophic factors (BDNF, GDNF, NGF, CNTF-like activity), promoting neuronal survival, synaptic plasticity, neurogenesis, and protection against excitotoxic and oxidative injury. Components are short-lived (e.g., BDNF-like activity ~10 minutes), so it is dosed as repeated courses rather than relying on accumulation.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 10-50 mL per day depending on indication; small-volume use up to 10 mL IV slow push or up to 5 mL IM over ~3 minutes; acute stroke 20-50 mL/day IV (diluted to at least 100 mL) for 10-21 days; TBI 20-50 mL/day IV for 7-30 days; Alzheimer's 10-30 mL 5 days/week for 4 weeks.
cycle: Course-based: 7-30 days for acute stroke/TBI, 4-week courses (often repeated) for dementia, frequently 5 days per week for chronic indications.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; possible injection-site reactions, dizziness, headache, agitation, sweating, flu-like symptoms, and rare hypersensitivity (avoid in severe renal impairment, epilepsy, or status epilepticus). Approved in many countries but not FDA-approved in the US; should be administered by a clinician with monitoring of renal function and seizure history.
comparisons: Compared with the small synthetic neuropeptides in this category (Semax, Selank, Adamax, Pinealon), Cerebrolysin is a clinically established multi-peptide protein mixture given by infusion under supervision with a far larger trial record (stroke, TBI, dementia), whereas the others are single research peptides usually self-administered intranasally or subcutaneously. Its effect sizes are modest and somewhat debated, but its safety and regulatory standing are stronger.

name: Adamax
summary: Adamax is a next-generation Semax derivative carrying N-terminal acetylation and a C-terminal adamantane group for greater stability and blood-brain-barrier penetration, researched for cognitive enhancement, neuroplasticity, and neuroprotection.
mechanism: The adamantane modification boosts lipophilicity so the peptide crosses the BBB more readily, and the acetyl/amidation guards against enzymatic degradation, giving an 8-10 hour half-life. Once central, it upregulates BDNF and increases TrkB receptor sensitivity, modulates dopamine, norepinephrine and serotonin, stabilizes microtubules through ADNP-derived mechanisms, and provides antioxidant and anti-inflammatory neuroprotection.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 200-300 mcg, once daily, or twice daily for enhanced neuroprotection.
cycle: 2-4 weeks per cycle.
sideEffects: Reported as mild and similar to Semax: overstimulation, headache, irritability, or insomnia if dosed late; data are very limited (about 4 studies). It is an experimental research compound, not approved for human use, with no established monitoring labs.
comparisons: Adamax is essentially an extended-duration, higher-penetration upgrade of Semax, trading Semax's short half-life and intranasal-only practicality for once-daily subcutaneous dosing; it is more stimulating and BDNF-focused than the anxiolytic Selank, and far less clinically validated than Cerebrolysin. It sits at the more experimental end of the cognitive-peptide spectrum.

name: Pinealon
summary: Pinealon is a synthetic Russian-developed tripeptide (Glu-Asp-Arg, the EDR peptide) studied for neuroprotection, cognitive enhancement, traumatic brain injury recovery, and age-related cognitive decline.
mechanism: As a short bioregulator peptide it penetrates cell and nuclear membranes and interacts directly with DNA, binding histone H1.3 and modulating gene expression, with activation of MAPK/ERK signalling. Downstream effects include caspase-3 suppression, reduced reactive oxygen species, protection against glutamate excitotoxicity, increased serotonergic activity, and improved NMDA-receptor-dependent spatial learning.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: injectable 5 mg daily for neuroprotection, or 100-300 mcg daily (or every other day) subcutaneously for cognitive enhancement; oral 0.2 mg twice daily on an empty stomach (morning and afternoon).
cycle: 20 days per cycle; for anti-aging use, 20-day courses repeated 2-3 times per year.
sideEffects: Reported as very well tolerated with an excellent safety profile over decades of Russian use; occasional injection-site reaction. It remains an experimental, non-FDA-approved research peptide; no specific monitoring labs are defined.
comparisons: Among cognitive peptides, Pinealon is a Khavinson-style short bioregulator acting at the gene-expression level, similar in concept to Epitalon and Cartalax, rather than a neurotransmitter-modulating nootropic like Semax/Selank; it is gentler and non-stimulating but has a thin Western evidence base and is often stacked with Epitalon for combined neuroprotective and telomere-related effects.

name: DSIP
summary: DSIP (delta sleep-inducing peptide) is a nine-amino-acid peptide first isolated from sleeping rabbits in the 1970s, studied for promoting deep slow-wave sleep, reducing stress, and modulating pain without classic sedative effects.
mechanism: It modulates several neurotransmitter systems, enhancing GABAergic tone, interacting with NMDA receptors, and modulating the endogenous opioid system, and it regulates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, lowering cortisol-driven stress responses. The net effect described is more delta-wave (slow-wave) sleep, shorter sleep latency, and fewer nocturnal awakenings without forcing sedation; its half-life is very short (~15 minutes in vitro), so it is dosed shortly before sleep.
dosing: Per peptidedosingprotocols.com: published 1980s human trials used 25 nmol/kg intravenously for short courses of about 4-7 nights; modern community research planning is subcutaneous at much lower, tolerability-based doses, typically a single evening dose 1-3 hours before sleep. peptide-db.com reports 100-300 mcg once daily.
cycle: Short cycles only, around 5-10 days, then a break.
sideEffects: Reported as mild: occasional headache, dizziness, transient grogginess, or mild GI upset; no tolerance or dependence reported. Not FDA-approved and currently on the FDA Category 2 do-not-compound list (PCAC review scheduled July 2026), so it is not for unsupervised human use; no specific monitoring labs are established.
comparisons: In the sleep category, DSIP is positioned as a non-sedating sleep-architecture modulator, contrasting with melatonin which shifts circadian timing rather than deepening slow-wave sleep, and with GH secretagogues like MK-677 whose sleep effect is a side benefit. Evidence is older and thin, and its regulatory status (Category 2) is the most restrictive of the common sleep aids.

name: Melatonin (injectable)
summary: Melatonin is the pineal sleep hormone (N-acetyl-5-methoxytryptamine); the injectable form delivers it parenterally to reinforce circadian timing, ease sleep onset, and is also explored for its antioxidant properties.
mechanism: Endogenous melatonin is released by the pineal gland in darkness and acts on MT1 and MT2 receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus to signal 'biological night,' shifting the sleep-wake phase, lowering core temperature, and promoting sleep onset. It is also a direct free-radical scavenger and modulates immune and HPA-axis activity. Injection bypasses first-pass metabolism, giving higher and more predictable systemic levels than oral dosing.
dosing: No source dump available; compiled. Oral research/clinical doses are typically 0.3-5 mg taken 30-60 minutes before bed; injectable use mirrors low single-digit milligram doses given in the evening, though there is no standardized parenteral protocol.
cycle: Used as needed or nightly during periods of disrupted sleep or jet lag; no formal on/off cycle, though continuous high-dose use is generally avoided.
sideEffects: Common: daytime drowsiness, vivid dreams, headache, dizziness; injection adds local site reactions. May lower blood pressure, interact with anticoagulants, sedatives, and immunosuppressants, and affect glucose tolerance at high doses. The injectable form is not an FDA-approved drug in the US; supervise use and review interacting medications rather than relying on lab monitoring.
comparisons: Within the sleep category, melatonin is the only true circadian hormone and is best for phase-shifting (jet lag, delayed sleep phase) rather than for deepening sleep, which is where DSIP is positioned; it is far better characterized and safer than DSIP, and unlike GH secretagogues used at night it has no effect on growth-hormone output. The injectable route offers little advantage over oral for routine sleep use.

name: Thymosin Alpha-1
summary: Thymosin Alpha-1 (TA1, sold as Zadaxin/thymalfasin) is a naturally occurring 28-amino-acid thymic peptide that trains and coordinates antiviral and antitumor immune cells; the synthetic form is approved in 35+ countries for chronic hepatitis B and C and as a cancer immune adjunct.
mechanism: TA1 activates Toll-like receptor pathways (notably TLR2/TLR9) on dendritic cells and monocytes, promotes maturation and differentiation of T cells, stimulates NK-cell activity, boosts antigen presentation and Th1 cytokine output, and at the same time dampens excessive pro-inflammatory cytokines (reductions on the order of 40-60% reported). Given subcutaneously it reaches roughly 90-95% bioavailability with a peak at about 2 hours and a ~2 hour half-life.
dosing: Per peptidedosingprotocols.com and peptide-db.com: the standard validated schedule is 1.6 mg subcutaneously twice weekly, 3-4 days apart, the dose used across the chronic hepatitis B/C trials and most cancer-adjunct, sepsis, and immune-support studies; a body-weight-adjusted protocol exists for adults under 40 kg.
cycle: Continuous twice-weekly dosing for 6-12 months in therapeutic (hepatitis) protocols; shorter courses in acute settings.
sideEffects: Exceptionally well tolerated, with <1% serious adverse events across 11,000+ studied patients; mild injection-site reactions, transient fatigue, or muscle aches are the main complaints. Not FDA-approved in the US (under 503A bulk-substance review) though approved abroad; clinicians typically watch the underlying condition (e.g., hepatitis labs, immune status) rather than TA1-specific markers.
comparisons: Among immune/thymic peptides, TA1 is by far the best-validated, with international drug approval and large RCTs, whereas Thymalin is an older calf-thymus extract (a peptide mixture rather than a defined molecule) backed mainly by Russian geroprotection studies; TA1 is also more immune-specific than Thymosin Beta-4 (tissue repair) and is the go-to when antiviral or anti-cancer immune support is the goal.

name: Thymalin
summary: Thymalin is a peptide bioregulator extracted from calf thymus gland, developed in the Soviet Union in the 1970s, used to restore and modulate immune function with reported geroprotective (anti-aging) and tissue-regenerative effects.
mechanism: It contains a mixture of short thymic peptides (e.g., KE, EW, EDP) that bind double-stranded DNA and histone proteins to regulate gene expression. Functionally it stimulates differentiation and activity of T-lymphocytes (especially CD4+ and CD8+), helps normalize cytokine balance, enhances antigen response, and stimulates stem-cell differentiation; it is also reported to influence gerontogene activity and immune-protein synthesis, with effects that persist for weeks after a short course.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 10 mg per injection, intramuscular or subcutaneous, daily for 5-10 days, then a break.
cycle: Short 5-10 day cycles; often repeated periodically and frequently combined with other bioregulators such as Epitalon/Epithalamin, with long-term studies running courses over several years.
sideEffects: Reported as well tolerated even in elderly populations, with the source noting essentially no side effects; possible mild injection-site reaction. It is an experimental, non-FDA-approved peptide extract whose strong claims (large mortality reductions) rest mainly on older Russian clinical studies; no specific monitoring labs are defined, though immune markers (CD4+, CD8+, NK cells) are tracked in studies.
comparisons: Versus Thymosin Alpha-1, Thymalin is an older, undefined multi-peptide thymus extract rather than a single approved molecule, and its evidence is largely Soviet/Russian geroprotection research rather than international RCTs; it overlaps with TA1 in promoting T-cell restoration but is positioned more as an anti-aging bioregulator (commonly paired with Epitalon) than as a targeted antiviral immune therapy.

name: FOXO4-DRI
summary: FOXO4-DRI is a D-retro-inverso senolytic peptide designed to selectively eliminate senescent 'zombie' cells that accumulate with age and impair tissue function. Headline use is experimental anti-aging and tissue rejuvenation.
mechanism: It competes with endogenous FOXO4 for binding to p53, disrupting the protective FOXO4-p53 interaction that keeps senescent cells alive. This excludes p53 from the nucleus and triggers cell-intrinsic apoptosis. The mechanism is relatively selective because the FOXO4-p53 interaction is a specific vulnerability of senescent cells while FOXO4 is minimally expressed in healthy tissue. The D-retro-inverso modification reverses the amino acids to increase potency and proteolytic stability.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: ~25-33 mg per injection, based on the original 2017 mouse study of 5 mg/kg IP (translating to roughly 25 mg for a 60 kg subject), given as 3 doses total on alternate days; self-experimenters report SubQ or IV. No established human dosing.
cycle: Per the source, one treatment cycle is 6 days total (Day 1, 3, 5), repeated only periodically.
sideEffects: Limited human data; generally tolerated in animal studies but this is a high-risk, experimental compound not approved for human use. Stop for allergic reactions or unexpected symptoms; theoretical concerns about off-target apoptosis and acute toxicity mean it should be regarded as research-only.
comparisons: Within longevity bioregulators, FOXO4-DRI is far more aggressive than gentle Khavinson peptides like epithalon, cartalax or vesugen, which work via epigenetic modulation rather than killing cells. It overlaps in goal with NAD+ and mitochondrial peptides (humanin, MOTS-c, SS-31) but is the only true senolytic in the set, carrying a correspondingly higher risk profile.

name: Epithalon
summary: Epithalon (Epitalon, AEDG) is a synthetic pineal tetrapeptide developed by Vladimir Khavinson, studied as a telomerase activator and anti-aging bioregulator. Headline use is cellular rejuvenation and restoration of melatonin/circadian rhythm.
mechanism: It activates telomerase at extremely low concentrations (10^-17 to 10^-15 M) to help maintain telomere length, stimulates melatonin production from the pineal gland, and modulates gene expression through epigenetic mechanisms. Animal work links it to neurogenesis support, immune/thymus regeneration and lifespan extension.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 100-500 mcg per injection SubQ once daily for 10-20 consecutive days (conservative 100 mcg, standard 200-500 mcg, Russian clinical equivalent ~300 mcg); a published human study used 0.5 mg/day sublingually for 20 days.
cycle: 10-20 day cycles with roughly 4-6 month breaks between cycles, repeated periodically.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated with minimal reported side effects; not an approved drug, so treat as experimental. Avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding and active cancer (theoretical telomerase/proliferation concern); discontinue for allergic reactions.
comparisons: Epithalon is the most-studied of the Khavinson short-peptide bioregulators (alongside cartalax for cartilage and vesugen for vasculature), with a unique telomerase/melatonin angle the others lack. Compared with NAD+ or mitochondrial peptides in the category it acts on telomere and pineal biology rather than energy metabolism, and is far gentler than the senolytic FOXO4-DRI.

name: Humanin
summary: Humanin (HN) was the first mitochondria-derived peptide discovered, a 24-amino-acid peptide encoded from the mitochondrial 16S rRNA region with neuroprotective, cytoprotective and anti-apoptotic properties. Headline research use is neuroprotection and mitochondrial/metabolic healthspan; most studies use the more potent analog HNG.
mechanism: Humanin signals through cell-surface receptors including FPRL1 and CNTFR, activating PI3K/AKT survival pathways. It blocks pro-apoptotic proteins such as Bax, reduces beta-amyloid toxicity in neurons, protects against oxidative stress and promotes mitochondrial biogenesis. Levels decline with age but remain high in long-lived species and in offspring of centenarians.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 1-5 mg per injection for native humanin, or 0.5-2 mg for the HNG analog, given 2-3 times weekly, SubQ (animal studies often use IP). peptidedosingprotocols.com notes there is no published human trial and that nearly all animal protocols use HNG, not native humanin, by the subcutaneous route.
cycle: Roughly 8-12 weeks of use for sustained mitochondrial support, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Limited human data; described as generally well tolerated in animal studies but it is an experimental, non-approved research peptide. No standard monitoring labs established; discontinue for allergic reactions or unexpected symptoms.
comparisons: Humanin is one of three mitochondrial-derived peptides in the category alongside MOTS-c (metabolic/AMPK focus) and SS-31 (cardiolipin focus); humanin leans neuroprotective and anti-apoptotic. It is commonly co-studied with NAD+ for broad mitochondrial support and is far lower-risk than the senolytic FOXO4-DRI.

name: MOTS-c
summary: MOTS-c is a 16-amino-acid mitochondrial-derived peptide that acts as a mitohormone, best known for activating AMPK and improving insulin sensitivity and metabolic flexibility. Headline use is metabolic support, exercise performance and anti-aging.
mechanism: MOTS-c inhibits the folate cycle, which raises AICAR and activates AMPK, the cell's key energy-sensing switch. Under metabolic stress it translocates to the nucleus and binds stress-response transcription factors (NRF2, ATF1/ATF7) to regulate metabolic and adaptive genes. It enhances mitochondrial biogenesis via PGC-1alpha and promotes fatty acid oxidation; exercise sharply increases endogenous skeletal-muscle MOTS-c.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: start ~5 mg daily SubQ, increasing to 10-15 mg based on goal — metabolic health 5-10 mg once daily, anti-aging 15 mg 3x weekly, exercise performance 10-15 mg pre-workout; morning/pre-exercise timing favored. peptidedosingprotocols.com describes the same subcutaneous, animal-study-derived structure.
cycle: Roughly 4-12 weeks depending on goal, per peptide-db.com.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated in research; an experimental, non-approved peptide. Useful markers to watch given its metabolic action are fasting glucose and HbA1c/insulin sensitivity; discontinue for allergic reactions or unexpected symptoms.
comparisons: Among the mitochondrial-derived peptides MOTS-c is the metabolic/AMPK specialist, versus humanin (neuroprotection) and SS-31 (cardiolipin protection). It overlaps functionally with NAD+ and AMPK-adjacent fat-loss compounds like 5-Amino-1MQ and AICAR, but is gentler and more rejuvenation-oriented than the senolytic FOXO4-DRI.

name: SS-31
summary: SS-31 (elamipretide) is an aromatic-cationic tetrapeptide that concentrates in the inner mitochondrial membrane and binds cardiolipin to protect mitochondrial structure and energy output. It is the active ingredient in FDA-approved Forzinity for Barth syndrome and is studied for mitochondrial disease, cardioprotection and anti-aging.
mechanism: SS-31 selectively binds cardiolipin, the phospholipid scaffold that organizes the electron transport chain, preventing cardiolipin peroxidation and stabilizing cristae structure under stress. This improves ATP production, reduces mitochondrial reactive oxygen species and protects against ischemia-reperfusion injury.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 5-10 mg once daily SubQ for general mitochondrial support, 10-20 mg pre-workout for athletic use, up to 40 mg once daily SubQ or IV in clinical protocols, and 0.25 mg/kg/hr continuous IV infusion for acute cardioprotection. peptidedosingprotocols.com notes the FDA-approved Forzinity product is 280 mg/3.5 mL (80 mg/mL) dosed per Barth-syndrome labeling, distinct from research-context dosing.
cycle: Roughly 4-12 weeks for therapeutic protocols, per peptide-db.com; light-sensitive and used in dark/amber vials.
sideEffects: Strong safety profile in clinical trials; main reported issue is injection-site reactions. As elamipretide it is now an approved drug for Barth syndrome but research-context use is off-label; protect from light and monitor injection sites.
comparisons: SS-31 is the cardiolipin/structure-focused member of the mitochondrial peptide trio, complementing MOTS-c (AMPK/metabolism) and humanin (neuroprotection), and is uniquely the only one with FDA approval. It pairs naturally with NAD+ for energy support and is far better characterized and lower-risk than the senolytic FOXO4-DRI.

name: Cartalax
summary: Cartalax is a synthetic AED tripeptide bioregulator developed by Vladimir Khavinson to support cartilage, connective tissue and fibroblast function. Headline use is joint health, cartilage maintenance and connective-tissue anti-aging.
mechanism: Cartalax modulates cell proliferation (raising Ki-67 expression in young and aged fibroblasts) while suppressing apoptosis, enhances collagen synthesis, inhibits matrix-degrading MMP-9, and reduces senescence markers to help preserve the extracellular matrix. Like other short Khavinson peptides its effects are thought to persist via epigenetic changes despite a minutes-long half-life.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 100-200 mcg orally (1-2 capsules) 1-2 times daily with meals, or roughly 700-1400 mcg in injectable form once daily.
cycle: Roughly 1-3 months per cycle depending on application, repeated periodically.
sideEffects: No side effects reported in Russian clinical use when taken properly; rare digestive upset. Not an approved drug, so experimental; avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding and active cancer (theoretical proliferation risk); discontinue for allergic reactions or no benefit after 3 months.
comparisons: Cartalax is the cartilage/connective-tissue specialist among the Khavinson bioregulators (epithalon = pineal/telomere, vesugen = vascular), and unlike them is often run alongside healing peptides like BPC-157 and TB-500. It is far gentler than the senolytic FOXO4-DRI and is not a systemic metabolic agent like NAD+ or the mitochondrial peptides.

name: Vesugen
summary: Vesugen is a Khavinson KED tripeptide bioregulator (lysine-glutamic acid-aspartic acid) developed at St. Petersburg's Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology, targeting the vascular system. Headline use is protecting blood vessels from age-related decline.
mechanism: Vesugen works via epigenetic regulation, interacting with DNA promoter regions and notably affecting Ki-67 (cell division) and sirtuin 1 (SIRT1) levels, a calorie-restriction-mimetic anti-aging protein. It limits atherosclerosis development, reduces endothelial dysfunction, enhances mesenchymal stem cell proliferation and may reverse the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP).
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: 10-20 mg per day as oral capsules (or ~10 mg injectable), daily for 10-20 consecutive days; maintenance ~10 mg in 2-3 cycles per year.
cycle: Standard 10-20 day cycle, repeated 2-3 times per year; effects said to persist post-cycle via epigenetic changes.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated with minimal reported side effects; experimental, not an approved drug. Avoid in active cardiovascular emergencies, known hypersensitivity, and pregnancy/breastfeeding; discontinue for allergic reactions or unusual cardiovascular symptoms.
comparisons: Vesugen is the vascular-system Khavinson bioregulator, complementary to epithalon (pineal/telomere) and cartalax (cartilage) and frequently combined with them in comprehensive bioregulator protocols. It is a gentle epigenetic agent rather than a metabolic powerhouse like NAD+ or a senolytic like FOXO4-DRI.

name: NAD+
summary: NAD+ is an essential cellular coenzyme central to energy production, DNA repair and sirtuin signaling whose levels fall with age; it is supplemented (or its precursors NMN/NR are taken) for energy, cognition and longevity. It is not a peptide but is grouped with peptide protocols for its injectable research-use format.
mechanism: Direct administration raises intracellular NAD+ available for ATP synthesis, for PARP-mediated DNA repair, and for sirtuin-driven longevity pathways, while supporting mitochondrial function and metabolic homeostasis. Oral NMN and NR work indirectly as biosynthetic precursors that the body converts to NAD+.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com: IV 250-1000 mg 1-2x weekly; IM/SubQ 100-500 mg 2-3x weekly; intranasal 25-50 mg 1-2x daily; oral NMN/NR precursors 100-500 mg daily. peptidedosingprotocols.com gives the same route-by-route structure (SubQ, IV, IM, oral NMN/NR, intranasal/sublingual, pre-mixed pens).
cycle: Roughly 4-12 weeks; IV therapy requires medical supervision.
sideEffects: Mild flushing during IV infusion and temporary nausea are common; fast infusion worsens flushing/cramping. Avoid alcohol during treatment (it depletes NAD+); IV use needs medical supervision; stop for severe injection-site reactions, persistent GI distress or allergic reactions.
comparisons: Unlike the peptide bioregulators in this category, NAD+ is a coenzyme with broad, well-publicized energy/longevity effects and many delivery routes including oral precursors. It is commonly stacked with mitochondrial peptides (SS-31, MOTS-c, humanin) for additive energetic support and with healing peptides like BPC-157/TB-500 for recovery; far broader-acting and lower-risk than the senolytic FOXO4-DRI.

name: PNC-27
summary: PNC-27 is an experimental chimeric anti-cancer peptide combining an HDM-2 (MDM2) binding domain from p53 (residues 12-26) with a cell-penetrating domain, designed in 2000 at SUNY Downstate. It selectively kills cancer cells in preclinical models and is not approved for human use.
mechanism: PNC-27's p53 segment binds membrane-bound HDM-2 (MDM2) that cancer cells uniquely express on their surface, inducing transmembrane pore formation and rapid tumor-cell necrosis (not apoptosis); it also enters cells and disrupts mitochondrial membranes. Normal cells lack surface HDM-2 and are reportedly spared, and the effect does not require functional p53 in the target cell.
dosing: Not established — peptide-db.com lists preclinical research only, with doses variable by study and routes limited to intraperitoneal or direct tumor injection in animal models; no approved human dosing exists.
cycle: Not established — no human clinical trials; protocols vary by research study.
sideEffects: Limited data, primarily preclinical; explicitly not approved for human use and should be treated as an experimental research peptide only. Cancers without membrane HDM-2 may not respond; no human safety or monitoring data exist.
comparisons: PNC-27 sits oddly within the longevity/bioregulator group as a cancer-cell-killing peptide; it shares a 'selective lethality' concept with the senolytic FOXO4-DRI (FOXO4-DRI clears senescent cells, PNC-27 lyses HDM-2-positive tumor cells) but targets a different cell population. Unlike NAD+ or the Khavinson peptides it has no healthspan or general anti-aging use and is purely investigational.

name: Follistatin 344
summary: Follistatin 344 (FS344) is an isoform of the natural follistatin protein/gene that acts as a potent myostatin antagonist, studied (largely as gene therapy or recombinant protein) to drive skeletal muscle growth and counter muscle wasting. It is experimental and not approved for human use.
mechanism: Follistatin binds and neutralizes myostatin (GDF-8) and related TGF-beta superfamily ligands such as activin A, preventing them from activating ActRIIB and downstream Smad2/3 signaling. Removing this brake on muscle protein synthesis and satellite-cell proliferation produces marked hypertrophy; the FS344 isoform has lower heparin/cell-surface affinity than FS315/FS288, affecting its circulating behavior.
dosing: No established human dosing; research has used AAV-delivered follistatin gene therapy (single intramuscular vector dose) in dystrophy/inclusion-body-myositis trials, and animal work with recombinant follistatin protein. Treat any black-market injectable 'follistatin' dosing as unvalidated.
cycle: Not defined for protein use; gene-therapy approaches are conceptually single-administration with durable expression. No legitimate cycling protocol exists.
sideEffects: Experimental and not approved; theoretical concerns include excessive/uneven muscle growth, tendon strain, effects on reproductive tissue (follistatin influences FSH and ovarian/testicular biology), and unknown long-term safety of forced systemic expression. No standard monitoring labs are established; regard as research-only.
comparisons: Within the myostatin/anabolic group, follistatin neutralizes myostatin by directly binding the ligand, whereas ACE-031 traps it with a soluble ActRIIB-Fc decoy receptor — conceptually synergistic but unstudied in combination, and ACE-031 has more human trial data (and a known vascular side-effect signal) than follistatin gene therapy. Both are far more aggressive and less characterized than mainstream anabolics.

name: ACE-031
summary: ACE-031 (ramatercept) is a soluble activin type IIB receptor fused to an IgG1 Fc domain that acts as a myostatin decoy receptor to promote muscle growth. Originally developed by Acceleron for Duchenne muscular dystrophy, it reached Phase 2 before development was halted over vascular side effects; it is experimental and not commercially available.
mechanism: ACE-031 mimics the extracellular domain of ActRIIB and acts as a ligand trap, intercepting myostatin (GDF-8), activin A, activin B and GDF-11 before they bind cell-surface receptors and activate Smad2/3 signaling. Blocking this pathway releases the natural brake on muscle protein synthesis and satellite-cell proliferation, producing rapid hypertrophy; the Fc fusion extends half-life via FcRn recycling and gives bivalent binding.
dosing: Per peptide-db.com (clinical research doses only): 0.1-3 mg/kg as a single IV infusion in healthy-volunteer Phase 1, and 0.5-2.5 mg/kg IV or SubQ every 2 weeks in the Phase 2 DMD trial; not commercially available and never approved.
cycle: Clinical trials used roughly 4-12 week treatment periods with dosing every 2 weeks; effects diminish over several weeks after the drug clears (half-life ~12-15 days).
sideEffects: Experimental and not approved. Phase 2 was stopped due to vascular adverse events — nosebleeds (epistaxis), gum bleeding and telangiectasia (dilated small vessels); monitor for bleeding events and skin vascular changes. Bone mineral density and body composition changes were also seen.
comparisons: ACE-031 is the receptor-decoy approach to myostatin inhibition, contrasting with follistatin's direct ligand-binding approach; it has the most human data in the category but also the clearest safety stop-signal (vascular bleeding). Conceptually synergistic with follistatin but unstudied in combination, and both are far more potent and riskier than conventional anabolic agents.

name: Dermorphin
summary: Dermorphin ([D-Ala2]-dermorphin) is a naturally occurring heptapeptide from Phyllomedusa frog skin and an extremely potent mu-opioid receptor agonist — by weight far stronger than morphine. WARNING: it is an opioid agonist with serious risk of respiratory depression, sedation, dependence and overdose; it is not approved or appropriate for human use, and is notorious as an illicit doping agent ('frog juice') in horse racing.
mechanism: Dermorphin selectively activates mu-opioid receptors with high affinity; the D-alanine at position 2 resists peptidase cleavage, giving unusual metabolic stability for a peptide. Mu-opioid agonism produces analgesia, euphoria, sedation and — critically — dose-dependent respiratory depression, the mechanism behind opioid overdose deaths, along with tolerance and physical dependence on repeated exposure.
dosing: No legitimate human dosing exists; not for human use. Animal/illicit reports describe small microgram-range injections producing profound opioid effects, but any use carries opioid-overdose and addiction risk and should be regarded as unsafe and, in many jurisdictions, illegal.
cycle: No safe cycle — repeated dosing causes rapid tolerance and physical dependence; there is no sanctioned protocol.
sideEffects: Respiratory depression (potentially fatal), profound sedation, miosis, nausea, constipation, euphoria, tolerance, physical dependence and withdrawal — the full opioid risk profile. It is not approved for human use; treat as a high-risk controlled-substance-class compound, not a research analgesic.
comparisons: Unlike the other entries in this group, dermorphin is a true opioid narcotic rather than a benign analgesic adjunct; it has no recovery, longevity or metabolic role. Compared with morphine it is dramatically more potent per milligram, which makes accidental overdose easier, and unlike clinical opioids it has no medical oversight, formulation standards or naloxone-paired safety framework.

name: Bacteriostatic Water
summary: Sterile water containing 0.9% benzyl alcohol as a preservative, used as the standard diluent for reconstituting lyophilized peptides in multi-dose vials.
mechanism: The benzyl alcohol acts as a bacteriostatic agent, inhibiting microbial growth so a reconstituted vial can be entered multiple times over a period of days to weeks. Chemically it is otherwise inert water that dissolves the lyophilized powder into an injectable solution. The mild preservative is what distinguishes it from plain sterile water and makes it suitable for repeated draws.
dosing: Drawn into a syringe and injected slowly down the inside wall of the peptide vial; typical reconstitution volumes are 1-3 mL per vial depending on the desired concentration. Once reconstituted, vials are generally refrigerated and used within about 28 days.
cycle: n/a
sideEffects: Benzyl alcohol is contraindicated in neonates and should be avoided in large volumes during pregnancy. Some people report mild stinging at the injection site. Not for intrathecal use. Composition is well established but this entry is compiled from general references.
comparisons: Versus sterile water for injection, bac water adds a preservative so a vial can be used repeatedly rather than once; versus 0.6% acetic acid, bac water is a neutral diluent for peptides that are already water-soluble, whereas acetic acid is reserved for poorly-soluble peptides that need an acidic solvent to fully dissolve.

name: Sterile Water for Injection
summary: Preservative-free sterile water intended for single-use reconstitution or dilution of injectable drugs and peptides.
mechanism: It is purified, sterilized, pyrogen-free water with no additives; it serves purely as a solvent to dissolve lyophilized powder or to dilute a concentrated solution. Because it contains no preservative, a vial reconstituted with it is meant to be used immediately or discarded. It is also used as a vehicle for compounding and as a final-stage rinse in sterile preparation.
dosing: Added to the peptide vial in volumes typically of 1-3 mL to achieve the target concentration; the reconstituted product should ideally be used the same day or within a short window if refrigerated.
cycle: n/a
sideEffects: Generally very safe, but injecting large volumes of plain water without electrolytes can theoretically cause hemolysis or electrolyte disturbance; it is meant for dilution, not bolus IV infusion on its own. Single-use only once opened. Compiled from general references.
comparisons: Versus bacteriostatic water, it lacks the 0.9% benzyl alcohol preservative, so it is preferred for single-dose use or for patients sensitive to benzyl alcohol but not suitable for multi-dose vials; versus 0.6% acetic acid, it is a neutral solvent that will not fully dissolve certain hard-to-solubilize peptides.

name: Acetic Acid (0.6%)
summary: A dilute (around 0.6%) acetic acid solution used as an acidic solvent to dissolve peptides that are poorly soluble in plain or bacteriostatic water, such as some BPC-157 or Tesamorelin preparations.
mechanism: Lowering the pH increases the solubility of certain peptides by protonating side chains and disrupting aggregation, allowing the lyophilized powder to go fully into solution where neutral water leaves it cloudy or undissolved. The acid concentration is kept low so the resulting solution is only mildly acidic and tolerable for subcutaneous injection. It otherwise acts as an inert aqueous vehicle once the peptide is dissolved.
dosing: Used in the same way as other diluents, drawn into a syringe and added to the vial in roughly 1-3 mL volumes; often only a fraction of acetic acid solution is needed, with the remainder topped up with bacteriostatic water. Many users only reach for it when standard diluents fail to clear the powder.
cycle: n/a
sideEffects: Can cause more stinging or burning at the injection site than neutral diluents due to the acidity; using too concentrated a solution risks irritation or tissue damage. Not a preservative, so any vial reconstituted with it alone should be treated as single-use unless preservative is added. Compiled from general references.
comparisons: Versus bacteriostatic and sterile water, acetic acid is the specialist option used only for peptides that will not dissolve in neutral solvents; it offers no preservative benefit and is generally less comfortable to inject, so it is not a default diluent.

name: Vitamin B12 (injectable)
summary: Injectable forms of vitamin B12 (cobalamin) used to correct deficiency and, in wellness settings, given as energy or recovery shots and as a component of lipotropic injection blends.
mechanism: Cobalamin is an essential cofactor for methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, supporting DNA synthesis, red blood cell formation, myelin maintenance, and homocysteine metabolism. Injected B12 bypasses gastrointestinal absorption, which is useful in pernicious anemia or malabsorption. Methylcobalamin and hydroxocobalamin are more active or longer-retained forms than the synthetic cyanocobalamin commonly used in clinics.
dosing: Typical IM doses range from about 1000 mcg given weekly to monthly for deficiency, with wellness clinics often using similar 1000 mcg shots; in lipotropic blends it is combined with compounds like methionine, inositol, choline, and L-carnitine in small IM volumes.
cycle: n/a or relevant note: can be used on an ongoing schedule for documented deficiency under medical supervision.
sideEffects: Generally very well tolerated; injection-site pain, mild diarrhea, or transient itching can occur, and rare hypersensitivity reactions are reported. High-dose unmonitored use is mostly wasteful rather than harmful since excess is excreted. Compiled from general references.
comparisons: Versus the true solvents in this category, B12 is an active nutrient rather than a vehicle; among its own forms, cyanocobalamin is cheapest and most common, methylcobalamin is a ready-to-use active form favored for neurological support, and hydroxocobalamin is retained longer in the body and used for cyanide poisoning.

name: HHB Blend
summary: A proprietary multi-peptide blend marketed by Synex/LELE-style suppliers; the exact composition is supplier-defined and not publicly documented.
mechanism: As a proprietary blend, its mechanism cannot be stated precisely because the constituent peptides and their ratios are not published. Blends in this general space typically combine recovery, anti-inflammatory, or performance peptides intended to act on tissue repair and systemic resilience. Any mechanistic claim should be treated as marketing until verified against the supplier's certificate of analysis.
dosing: No standardized dosing exists publicly; users are directed to follow the dosing and reconstitution instructions on the specific supplier's label or COA. Treat any third-party protocol with caution.
cycle: n/a — no established cycling guidance; defer to supplier documentation.
sideEffects: Composition is not publicly verified, so the side-effect and interaction profile is unknown; risks depend entirely on the undisclosed ingredients and their purity. Confirm contents, sterility, and dosing with the supplier's COA before use. Compiled from general references.
comparisons: Unlike single named peptides, HHB cannot be meaningfully compared on mechanism or potency because its formula is undisclosed; the only sound comparison is that named, well-characterized peptides offer transparency that proprietary blends like this do not.

name: SHB Blend
summary: A proprietary multi-peptide blend sold by Synex/LELE-style suppliers; the exact composition is supplier-defined and not publicly documented.
mechanism: Because the component peptides and ratios are not disclosed, no reliable mechanism can be described. Products in this category are generally promoted for recovery, anti-inflammatory, or wellness effects via combinations of repair and signaling peptides. Any stated mechanism should be confirmed against the supplier's certificate of analysis rather than taken at face value.
dosing: There is no public standardized dose; rely on the dosing, concentration, and reconstitution instructions provided on the supplier's label or COA, and be skeptical of unofficial protocols.
cycle: n/a — no established cycling guidance; defer to supplier documentation.
sideEffects: Composition is not publicly verified, so the safety, side-effect, and drug-interaction profile is unknown and depends on the undisclosed ingredients and their quality. Verify contents, sterility, and dosing with the supplier's COA before use. Compiled from general references.
comparisons: SHB cannot be benchmarked against named peptides on efficacy or safety because its formula is hidden; the meaningful contrast is that transparent single-agent peptides allow informed dosing whereas this proprietary blend does not.

name: ATM-15 Blend
summary: A proprietary multi-peptide blend offered by Synex/LELE-style suppliers; the exact composition is supplier-defined and not publicly documented.
mechanism: With the constituent peptides and their proportions undisclosed, its mechanism of action cannot be stated with confidence. Such blends are typically marketed around recovery, tissue repair, anti-inflammatory, or general wellness pathways using a mix of signaling peptides. Treat any specific mechanistic claim as unverified until checked against the supplier's certificate of analysis.
dosing: No publicly standardized dosing is available; follow the dosing and reconstitution guidance on the specific supplier's label or COA and approach third-party protocols cautiously.
cycle: n/a — no established cycling guidance; defer to supplier documentation.
sideEffects: Composition is not publicly verified, so its side-effect and interaction profile is unknown and hinges on the undisclosed ingredients and their purity. Confirm exact contents, sterility, and dosing with the supplier's COA before any use. Compiled from general references.
comparisons: ATM-15 cannot be compared on potency or safety with characterized peptides because its formula is proprietary; the only firm comparison is that named, documented peptides provide the transparency this blend lacks.
